shortDescription: The igniter circuit for the number 1 ignition coil is not functioning correctly.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates a problem with the number 1 ignition coil igniter circuit. Toyota's ignition system uses individual coil-on-plug assemblies with integrated igniters that amplify the PCM's ignition signal to fire the spark plug.

When the igniter circuit fails, the number 1 cylinder will not receive spark, causing a misfire. The engine will run rough, lose power proportional to the missing cylinder, and emit unburned fuel through the exhaust, which can damage the catalytic converter.

Common causes include a failed ignition coil assembly, a damaged coil connector, or a wiring issue between the PCM and the coil. Toyota ignition coils are generally reliable but can fail after 80,000-120,000 miles, especially if spark plugs have not been replaced on schedule.
safetyImpact: Misfire on one cylinder causes reduced power and can overheat the catalytic converter with unburned fuel.

causes.cause: Worn or fouled spark plug on cylinder 1

symptoms: Check Engine Light on or flashing

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil Test
fixSteps.instruction: Swap the cylinder 1 ignition coil with another cylinder's coil. Clear codes and run the engine. If the misfire moves to the other cylinder, the coil is faulty. If it stays on cylinder 1, the problem is the plug, wiring, or PCM.

fixSteps.title: Check Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and inspect the cylinder 1 spark plug. Look for wear, fouling, cracking, or incorrect gap. Replace if needed.

fixSteps.title: Replace Ignition Coil
fixSteps.instruction: If the coil test confirmed a faulty coil, replace it. Consider replacing all coils if they are of similar age and mileage to prevent future failures.

faq.q: Should I replace all the coils at once?
faq.a: If one coil has failed and the others are the same age, they may fail soon too. Many mechanics recommend replacing all coils as a set for reliability, but it is not strictly necessary.

faq.q: Can a bad spark plug cause P1300?
faq.a: While a bad spark plug primarily causes misfire codes (P0301), a severely fouled or shorted plug can damage the ignition coil, eventually triggering P1300.

title: Toyota - Igniter Circuit Malfunction (No. 2)
shortDescription: The igniter circuit for the number 2 ignition coil is not functioning correctly.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates a malfunction in the number 2 ignition coil igniter circuit. It is the cylinder 2 equivalent of P1300, with identical diagnostic and repair procedures.

The igniter circuit includes the coil-on-plug assembly, its electrical connector, the wiring back to the PCM, and the PCM's igniter driver transistor. A failure at any point in this circuit will prevent cylinder 2 from firing.

On Toyota 4-cylinder engines, cylinder 2 is typically the second from the timing belt/chain end. On V6 engines, the cylinder numbering follows Toyota's firing order convention. Consult your service manual for cylinder identification on your specific engine.
safetyImpact: Single cylinder misfire reduces power and may damage the catalytic converter.

causes.cause: Worn or fouled spark plug on cylinder 2

symptoms: Check Engine Light on or flashing

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil to Confirm
fixSteps.instruction: Swap the cylinder 2 coil with another cylinder. Clear codes. If the misfire follows the coil, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and check the cylinder 2 spark plug. Replace if worn, fouled, or damaged.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new ignition coil on cylinder 2. Clear codes and verify the misfire is resolved.

faq.q: What is a coil swap test?
faq.a: A coil swap test involves moving the suspected bad coil to a different cylinder. If the misfire moves with the coil, you have confirmed the coil is the problem. This is the quickest way to diagnose ignition coil issues.

faq.q: Can I drive with one cylinder misfiring?
faq.a: Brief driving to a shop is acceptable, but prolonged driving with a misfire can damage the catalytic converter. If the Check Engine Light is flashing, the damage risk is high and you should stop driving.

title: Toyota - Igniter Circuit Malfunction (No. 3)
shortDescription: The igniter circuit for the number 3 ignition coil is not functioning correctly.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates a failure in the cylinder 3 ignition coil igniter circuit. As with P1300 and P1305, the most likely cause is a failed ignition coil, with a worn spark plug being the second most common cause.

On inline 4-cylinder Toyota engines, cylinder 3 is the third from the timing end. On V6 engines (1MZ-FE, 2GR-FE, etc.), cylinder 3 is typically on the front bank. The coil swap diagnostic approach is identical to other igniter codes in this series.
safetyImpact: Misfire reduces power and risks catalytic converter damage from unburned fuel.
symptoms: Check Engine Light on or flashing

fixSteps.title: Coil Swap Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Swap the cylinder 3 coil with another cylinder. Clear codes and see if the misfire follows the coil.

fixSteps.title: Check Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and replace the cylinder 3 spark plug if worn or fouled.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty ignition coil. Clear codes and verify proper operation.

faq.q: All my coils are original with 120,000 miles. Should I replace them all?
faq.a: If one has failed at that mileage, the others are likely near the end of their life as well. Replacing all four or six coils as a set with new spark plugs is a sound preventive measure.

faq.q: Why is the igniter integrated into the coil on Toyota engines?
faq.a: Toyota's coil-on-plug design integrates the igniter (amplifier) into each coil assembly for a compact, efficient design. This means when the igniter fails, the entire coil unit is replaced.

title: Toyota - Igniter Circuit Malfunction (No. 4)
shortDescription: The igniter circuit for the number 4 ignition coil is not functioning correctly.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates a failure in the cylinder 4 ignition coil igniter circuit. On 4-cylinder engines, this is the last cylinder, and on V6 engines, it is the first cylinder on bank 2.

The diagnostic and repair procedures are identical to P1300, P1305, and P1310. Perform a coil swap test, check the spark plug, and replace the faulty component. If all four igniter codes appear simultaneously, suspect a common wiring issue, PCM failure, or ignition system power supply problem rather than four individual coil failures.
safetyImpact: Single cylinder misfire causes power loss and catalytic converter risk.
symptoms: Check Engine Light on or flashing

fixSteps.title: Coil Swap Test
fixSteps.instruction: Swap cylinder 4 coil with another cylinder and check if the misfire follows.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Check cylinder 4 spark plug condition and replace if necessary.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty ignition coil, clear codes, and verify repair.

faq.q: I have P1300, P1305, P1310, and P1315 all at once. Is that possible?
faq.a: All four igniter codes together usually means a common cause rather than four failed coils. Check the main ignition power supply, the ground circuit, and the PCM. A single wiring or fuse issue could affect all coils.

faq.q: Which Toyota engines use individual igniter codes?
faq.a: Most Toyota engines with coil-on-plug ignition use these codes, including the 1ZZ-FE, 2AZ-FE, 1MZ-FE, 2GR-FE, and others. Older distributor-based engines use different igniter codes.

title: GM - Crankshaft/Camshaft Position Correlation Error
shortDescription: The relationship between the crankshaft and camshaft position signals is outside the expected range.
fullDescription:
This GM/Chevrolet-specific code indicates that the PCM has detected an abnormal correlation between the crankshaft position (CKP) sensor and the camshaft position (CMP) sensor signals. The PCM uses both signals to determine engine timing and cylinder identification. When the signals do not align within expected parameters, this code is set.

On GM V8 engines (LS-series, Vortec, etc.), this code is commonly caused by a worn distributor gear (on distributor-equipped engines), a stretched timing chain, or a misaligned distributor. On newer engines without distributors, a stretched timing chain or failed timing chain tensioner is the most common cause.

This code can cause hard starting, rough running, reduced power, and in severe cases, engine damage if the timing is significantly off.
safetyImpact: Significant timing deviation can cause engine damage and reduced power. May cause stalling in severe cases.

causes.cause: Failed timing chain tensioner or guide

fixSteps.title: Monitor Sensor Signals
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor the CKP and CMP sensor signals and correlation value. Note the degree of deviation from specification.

fixSteps.title: Check Distributor (if applicable)
fixSteps.instruction: On engines with distributors, check the distributor for excessive shaft play or a worn drive gear. If the gear teeth are rounded or chipped, replace the distributor. Ensure proper alignment with a timing light.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Chain
fixSteps.instruction: On chain-driven engines, check for timing chain slack by removing the timing cover or using a borescope. A chain with excessive slack needs replacement along with the tensioner and guides.

fixSteps.title: Replace Timing Components
fixSteps.instruction: If the timing chain is stretched, replace the chain, tensioner, and guides as a complete kit. This is a major repair that requires proper engine timing alignment. Clear codes and verify proper correlation after repair.

faq.q: Can I just adjust the distributor to fix P1345?
faq.a: If the cause is a slightly misaligned distributor, repositioning it may work. However, if the distributor gear is worn or the timing chain is stretched, adjustment alone will not fix the root cause.

faq.q: Is a timing chain replacement expensive?
faq.a: Timing chain replacement on GM V8 engines typically costs $800-1500 at a shop. The parts are relatively affordable but the labor is significant as the front of the engine must be partially disassembled.

title: Toyota - VVT Sensor / Camshaft Position Sensor Range/Performance (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The variable valve timing position sensor on bank 1 is reading outside the expected range.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates that the VVT (Variable Valve Timing) position sensor on bank 1 is reporting a camshaft position that is outside the PCM's expected range for the current operating conditions. The VVT system adjusts camshaft timing for optimal performance and emissions at different engine speeds.

The VVT sensor detects the actual position of the camshaft relative to the crankshaft. If the actual position does not match the PCM's commanded position within a defined tolerance, this code is set. This may indicate that the VVT actuator is not responding correctly or the sensor itself is faulty.

Common causes include a stuck VVT actuator, clogged oil passages to the VVT system, low oil pressure, or a faulty VVT sensor.
safetyImpact: Incorrect valve timing can reduce power and efficiency but typically does not pose an immediate safety hazard.

causes.cause: Stuck or malfunctioning VVT actuator (cam phaser)
Clogged oil control valve or oil passages
Low oil level or degraded oil
Stretched timing chain affecting VVT range

fixSteps.title: Check Oil Level and Condition
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the oil level is correct and the oil is not excessively degraded. VVT systems are very sensitive to oil quality. If the oil is dirty or low, perform an oil change with the correct grade specified by Toyota.

fixSteps.title: Inspect VVT Oil Control Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the VVT oil control valve (OCV) and inspect its screen for sludge or debris. Clean or replace the OCV. Test its operation by applying 12V to verify the plunger moves freely.

fixSteps.title: Test VVT Actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to command VVT advance and retard while monitoring actual vs commanded camshaft position. If the actuator does not respond, it may be stuck and need replacement.

fixSteps.title: Replace VVT Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty OCV, VVT actuator, or sensor as indicated by testing. Clear codes and verify proper VVT operation across the RPM range.

faq.q: Can an oil change fix P1346?
faq.a: Sometimes, especially if the oil is very degraded or the wrong viscosity. VVT systems depend on clean oil of the correct grade. An oil change is always the first step in diagnosing VVT issues.

faq.q: What is the startup rattle associated with VVT problems?
faq.a: The rattle heard on startup is often caused by a worn VVT actuator or low oil pressure in the VVT passages. When the engine is off, oil drains from the actuator, and on startup, there is a brief period of slack before oil pressure builds up.

title: Toyota - VVT System Malfunction (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The variable valve timing system on bank 1 is not operating within expected parameters.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates a general malfunction of the VVT system on bank 1. Unlike P1346 which specifically addresses the sensor range, P1349 is a broader system performance code that can indicate issues with the VVT actuator, oil control valve, or the hydraulic circuit that drives the VVT mechanism.

The VVT-i (Variable Valve Timing with intelligence) system is a core part of Toyota's engine management strategy. It continuously adjusts intake camshaft timing to optimize power, fuel economy, and emissions. When the system cannot adjust timing as commanded, engine performance suffers across the entire operating range.

This code is particularly common on higher-mileage Toyota vehicles that have not received regular oil changes with the correct specification oil. The fine oil passages in the VVT system are easily clogged by sludge.
safetyImpact: Reduced engine performance may affect acceleration capability but is not typically dangerous.

causes.cause: Oil sludge blocking VVT oil passages

symptoms: Stalling at idle in some cases

fixSteps.title: Oil Service
fixSteps.instruction: Change the engine oil and filter using the exact specification Toyota recommends (typically 0W-20 or 5W-30). Use a quality oil filter. This alone resolves P1349 in some cases.

fixSteps.title: Clean or Replace Oil Control Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the VVT oil control valve from the cylinder head. Clean the screen filter and valve body. If the valve is stuck or does not click when 12V is applied, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Test System Response
fixSteps.instruction: After cleaning/replacement, clear codes and use a scan tool to monitor VVT actuator position vs commanded position. The actual position should closely follow the commanded position.

faq.q: How important are regular oil changes for the VVT system?
faq.a: Extremely important. Toyota VVT systems use very fine oil passages that are easily clogged by sludge. Skipping oil changes or using the wrong oil grade is the leading cause of VVT problems on Toyota engines.

faq.q: What oil should I use for my Toyota with VVT?
faq.a: Check your owner's manual, but most modern Toyota engines require 0W-20 full synthetic oil. Using a heavier oil can impair VVT operation.

title: Honda - Top Dead Center Sensor Intermittent Interruption
shortDescription: The TDC (top dead center) sensor signal is experiencing intermittent dropouts.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates that the TDC (Top Dead Center) sensor signal is cutting in and out intermittently. Honda engines use a TDC sensor (sometimes integrated with the crankshaft position sensor) to determine the precise position of the number 1 piston for ignition timing and fuel injection sequencing.

Intermittent interruptions in the TDC signal can cause momentary misfires, stumbles, or in severe cases, a no-start condition. The engine may run fine most of the time but occasionally stumble or hesitate.

The TDC sensor is typically located near the crankshaft pulley or in the distributor on older Honda models. The sensor and its wiring are exposed to engine heat and vibration, which can cause connector failures and wire breaks over time.
safetyImpact: Intermittent signal loss can cause momentary engine stumbles that could be hazardous during acceleration.

causes.cause: Damaged or corroded TDC sensor connector
Broken or chafed wiring in the TDC sensor circuit
Excessive crankshaft endplay affecting sensor gap

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the TDC sensor and disconnect its connector. Inspect for corrosion, bent pins, or moisture. Clean with electrical contact cleaner and apply dielectric grease.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wiring from the TDC sensor to the PCM connector. Look for chafing, breaks, or heat damage. Wiggle the harness while monitoring the sensor signal on a scanner to find intermittent breaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace TDC Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the wiring is good and the connector is clean, replace the TDC sensor. Verify proper air gap between the sensor and the reluctor wheel (if adjustable). Clear codes and test drive.

faq.q: What is the difference between the TDC sensor and the CKP sensor on Honda?
faq.a: On many Honda engines, the TDC and CKP sensors are separate sensors that read different reluctor wheels. The CKP sensor provides RPM and crankshaft angle data, while the TDC sensor specifically identifies the cylinder 1 TDC position for fuel and ignition sequencing.

faq.q: Can a bad TDC sensor cause a no-start?
faq.a: Yes. If the TDC signal is completely lost, the PCM may not be able to determine when to fire the ignition or inject fuel, causing a no-start condition.

title: Honda - Top Dead Center Sensor No Signal
shortDescription: The PCM is not receiving any signal from the TDC sensor.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates a complete loss of the TDC sensor signal. Unlike P1361 which is intermittent, P1362 means the signal is entirely absent. This is a more severe condition that will likely prevent the engine from running or cause it to die immediately after starting.

The PCM needs the TDC signal to sequence fuel injection and ignition events to the correct cylinders. Without this signal, the engine cannot maintain proper combustion timing.

This code requires immediate diagnosis. Check the TDC sensor connector first, then the sensor itself, and finally the wiring back to the PCM.
safetyImpact: Complete signal loss can cause sudden engine stalling, which is hazardous in traffic.

causes.cause: Broken wiring in the TDC circuit
Damaged reluctor wheel or trigger plate

symptoms: Engine does not start or dies immediately
No ignition or fuel injection signal
Engine cranks but will not fire

fixSteps.title: Verify Sensor Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Check that the TDC sensor is plugged in and the connector is not damaged. Reconnect if loose.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Output
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter set to AC millivolts, check for a signal output from the TDC sensor while cranking the engine. A working sensor will produce an AC voltage signal. No signal means the sensor has failed.

fixSteps.title: Replace TDC Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the TDC sensor. Clear codes, start the engine, and verify proper operation.

faq.q: My Honda cranks but won't start. Could it be the TDC sensor?
faq.a: Yes, P1362 is a common cause of crank-no-start on Honda vehicles. The engine needs the TDC signal to know when to inject fuel and fire the spark plugs.

faq.q: Where is the TDC sensor located on my Honda?
faq.a: On most Honda engines, the TDC sensor is located near the crankshaft pulley area, often mounted to the engine block or timing cover. On older models with distributors, it may be inside the distributor.

title: Honda - Camshaft Position Sensor Intermittent Interruption
shortDescription: The camshaft position sensor signal is experiencing intermittent interruptions.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates that the CMP (Camshaft Position) sensor signal is dropping out intermittently. Honda uses the CMP sensor to monitor camshaft position for fuel injection timing and VTEC control. Intermittent loss of this signal can cause momentary misfires and unstable engine operation.

The CMP sensor on most Honda engines is a Hall-effect or magnetic reluctor type sensor mounted near the camshaft. It reads a trigger pattern on the camshaft or camshaft drive gear to provide position information to the PCM.

This code often accompanies VTEC-related codes because the PCM uses CMP data to verify VTEC engagement. If the CMP signal is unreliable, the PCM may disable VTEC as a protective measure.
safetyImpact: Intermittent signal loss may cause brief stumbles or misfires during driving.

causes.cause: Corroded or loose CMP sensor connector
Damaged wiring near the CMP sensor

fixSteps.title: Inspect CMP Sensor Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the CMP sensor on the cylinder head. Disconnect and inspect the connector for corrosion or damage. Clean and apply dielectric grease.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wiring harness for the CMP sensor. Look for rubbed-through insulation, heat damage, or pinched wires. Wiggle test while monitoring the signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace CMP Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the wiring is good, replace the CMP sensor. Clear codes and test drive to verify the intermittent signal is resolved.

faq.q: Can P1381 affect VTEC?
faq.a: Yes. The PCM uses the CMP signal to verify VTEC operation. If the CMP signal is unreliable, the PCM may disable VTEC as a safety measure, resulting in loss of high-RPM power.

faq.q: Is the CMP sensor the same as the TDC sensor?
faq.a: No. On Honda engines, the CMP and TDC sensors are separate components. The CMP reads the camshaft position, while the TDC reads the crankshaft position for cylinder 1 identification.

title: Honda - Camshaft Position Sensor No Signal
shortDescription: The PCM is not receiving any signal from the camshaft position sensor.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates a complete absence of the camshaft position sensor signal. This is the more severe version of P1381. Without the CMP signal, the PCM loses its ability to precisely time fuel injection events relative to valve position and cannot verify camshaft timing.

While the engine may still run using the crankshaft position sensor for basic timing reference, performance will be degraded and VTEC will be disabled. In some cases, the engine may not start at all, depending on the Honda model and year.

This code demands immediate attention. The most common cause is a failed CMP sensor, but a broken wire or disconnected connector should be ruled out first.
safetyImpact: May cause engine stalling or prevent starting, creating a stranding hazard.

causes.cause: Broken wiring in the CMP circuit

symptoms: Rough running if engine does start

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the CMP sensor is connected. If it was accidentally disconnected during other work, reconnect it.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Test the CMP sensor for proper voltage supply (typically 5V reference), ground, and signal output. A sensor with no output while cranking has failed.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the CMP sensor. Ensure the O-ring seal is properly seated. Clear codes and verify proper signal with a scanner.

faq.q: Can my Honda run without a CMP signal?
faq.a: Some Honda models can run in a degraded mode using only the CKP/TDC sensors, but performance will be poor and VTEC will be disabled. Other models may not start at all without the CMP signal.

faq.q: I replaced the valve cover gasket and now have P1382. What happened?
faq.a: The CMP sensor connector may have been accidentally disconnected or damaged during the valve cover gasket replacement. Check the connector first.

title: BMW - Camshaft Position Sensor (Exhaust) Signal Error
shortDescription: The exhaust camshaft position sensor signal is erratic or out of expected range.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates that the DME has detected an irregular signal from the exhaust camshaft position sensor. BMW VANOS systems use separate sensors for the intake and exhaust camshafts to monitor and control variable valve timing independently.

An erratic exhaust cam sensor signal can prevent the DME from properly controlling the exhaust VANOS actuator, leading to incorrect exhaust valve timing. This affects engine efficiency, emissions, and can cause rough running, especially at idle and low RPMs.

The exhaust camshaft sensor is a Hall-effect type mounted on the cylinder head near the exhaust camshaft gear. It reads a multi-toothed target wheel to provide position and speed data to the DME.
safetyImpact: Incorrect exhaust valve timing reduces performance but does not typically pose an immediate safety concern.

causes.cause: Damaged sensor wiring near exhaust manifold heat
Worn VANOS exhaust actuator affecting sensor target

symptoms: Loss of power at certain RPMs

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the exhaust camshaft position sensor on the cylinder head. Inspect the connector for oil contamination, corrosion, or heat damage. The exhaust side is more prone to heat-related failures.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Using a BMW-compatible scanner, monitor the exhaust cam sensor signal quality. The signal should be clean and consistent. Erratic readings confirm a sensor issue.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the exhaust camshaft position sensor. BMW sensors are typically held in with a single bolt. Use an OEM or quality sensor. Clear codes and verify the signal is clean.

faq.q: Is the exhaust cam sensor different from the intake cam sensor?
faq.a: On BMW VANOS engines, yes. The intake and exhaust camshafts each have their own position sensor. They may look similar but are mounted at different locations on the cylinder head.

faq.q: Can a bad cam sensor cause VANOS problems?
faq.a: A faulty cam sensor will prevent the DME from accurately controlling the VANOS actuator for that camshaft, leading to symptoms similar to VANOS mechanical failure. Always check sensors before assuming the VANOS unit itself is faulty.

title: GM - EGR Valve Stuck Open
shortDescription: The exhaust gas recirculation valve is stuck in the open position.
fullDescription:
This GM/Chevrolet-specific code indicates that the PCM has detected the EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) valve is stuck in the open position. The EGR system reduces nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions by recirculating a small amount of exhaust gas back into the intake manifold to lower combustion temperatures.

When the EGR valve is stuck open, exhaust gas is constantly flowing into the intake, diluting the fresh air-fuel mixture. This causes rough idle, stalling, and poor performance because the engine is receiving too much inert gas that does not support combustion, especially at low speeds and idle where the EGR should be closed.

GM vehicles commonly experience carbon buildup on the EGR valve pintle and seat, preventing it from closing completely. The electronic EGR valves used on many GM vehicles can also fail due to a faulty stepper motor or position sensor.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall at idle, which is hazardous in traffic. Rough running reduces driving confidence.

causes.cause: Carbon deposits preventing EGR valve from closing
Failed EGR valve motor or solenoid
Vacuum leak holding EGR valve open (vacuum-operated models)

symptoms: Engine stalling at idle or low speeds

fixSteps.title: Inspect EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the EGR valve and inspect the pintle and seat for carbon buildup. Check if the valve can close completely. Heavy carbon deposits are visible as a black crusty buildup.

fixSteps.title: Clean EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Soak the EGR valve in carburetor cleaner and use a small wire brush to remove carbon deposits from the pintle and seat. Ensure the valve moves freely. Also clean the EGR passages in the intake manifold.

fixSteps.title: Test EGR Valve Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Reinstall the EGR valve. Use a scan tool to command the EGR valve open and closed while monitoring its position. The valve should respond smoothly and close completely when commanded.

fixSteps.title: Replace EGR Valve if Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not restore proper operation, replace the EGR valve. Use a new gasket. Clear codes and verify the valve closes fully at idle.

faq.q: Can I just clean the EGR valve instead of replacing it?
faq.a: Yes, in many cases cleaning restores proper operation. If the carbon buildup is not severe and the valve motor/solenoid still works, cleaning is an effective and low-cost repair.

faq.q: Why does the EGR valve get clogged with carbon?
faq.a: The EGR valve passes hot exhaust gas containing carbon particles. Over time, these deposits accumulate on the valve and in the passages. Short trips and city driving accelerate carbon buildup because the engine never fully heats up to burn off deposits.

title: BMW - Secondary Air System Bank 1 Flow Too Low
shortDescription: The secondary air injection system is not delivering sufficient airflow to bank 1 of the exhaust.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates that the secondary air injection (SAI) system is not providing enough airflow to the bank 1 exhaust during cold start. The SAI system pumps fresh air into the exhaust manifold during the first 1-2 minutes of engine operation to help the catalytic converter reach operating temperature quickly and reduce cold-start emissions.

BMW SAI systems are known for reliability issues, particularly on the N52 and N54 engines. The most common failure point is the SAI valve, which can stick closed due to carbon buildup or moisture corrosion. The SAI pump itself can also fail, and the check valve can leak, allowing exhaust gases to damage the pump.

Many BMW owners are familiar with the secondary air system fault code as it is one of the most common BMW Check Engine Light causes.
safetyImpact: No direct safety impact. The vehicle operates normally except for increased cold-start emissions.

causes.cause: Stuck or failed secondary air injection valve
Failed SAI check valve allowing exhaust backflow

symptoms: Brief rough running during cold start (first 1-2 minutes)
Louder exhaust note during cold start

fixSteps.title: Listen for SAI Pump
fixSteps.instruction: On a cold start, listen for the SAI pump (a brief whirring or blowing sound lasting about 90 seconds). If no sound is heard, the pump or its relay may have failed. If the pump runs but there is no effect, the valve or piping is blocked.

fixSteps.title: Test SAI Valve
fixSteps.instruction: The SAI valve is vacuum-operated on most BMW models. Apply vacuum to the valve actuator and verify it opens. If it does not open, replace the valve. Check the vacuum supply line as well.

fixSteps.title: Test SAI Pump
fixSteps.instruction: Apply 12V directly to the SAI pump to verify it runs. Check for adequate airflow output. If the pump is weak or does not run, replace it. Also check the relay in the engine compartment fuse box.

fixSteps.title: Replace Failed Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty SAI valve, pump, check valve, or relay as identified. Clear codes with a BMW-compatible scan tool. The code should not reappear after the next cold start cycle.

faq.q: Can I delete the secondary air system on my BMW?
faq.a: While some aftermarket solutions exist to code out the SAI system, this will cause the vehicle to fail emissions testing in states that require it. The SAI system is a legal emissions requirement.

faq.q: Why is the SAI system only active during cold start?
faq.a: The SAI system's sole purpose is to help the catalytic converter light off (reach operating temperature) quickly during cold starts. Once the converter is hot, the SAI system shuts off because it is no longer needed.

title: Ford - Unable to Bleed Up Fuel Tank Vacuum
shortDescription: The EVAP system is unable to relieve excessive vacuum in the fuel tank.
fullDescription:
This Ford-specific code indicates that the PCM has detected excessive vacuum in the fuel tank that the EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) system cannot bleed off. The EVAP system manages fuel vapors and maintains proper fuel tank pressure. When the tank develops excessive vacuum, it can collapse the tank, cause fuel delivery issues, and affect the fuel gauge.

The most common cause on Ford vehicles is a stuck-closed canister vent valve (CVV) that prevents outside air from entering the fuel tank to equalize pressure as fuel is consumed. A clogged EVAP canister or a kinked vent hose can also cause this condition.

This code is particularly common on Ford trucks (F-150, F-250) and the Escape/Focus models. You may notice a loud whooshing sound when removing the fuel cap, which is the excessive vacuum being released.
safetyImpact: Excessive fuel tank vacuum can stress the fuel tank and cause fuel delivery issues in extreme cases, but is generally not dangerous.

causes.cause: Kinked or blocked EVAP vent hose

symptoms: Loud whooshing sound when removing fuel cap
Difficulty refueling (pump nozzle clicks off repeatedly)

fixSteps.title: Test Canister Vent Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the canister vent valve (typically near the charcoal canister under the vehicle or near the fuel tank). Apply 12V to the valve and listen for a click. With no power, the valve should be open (you can blow through it). When powered, it should close (no airflow). If stuck closed, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Check all EVAP vent hoses and lines for kinks, cracks, or blockages. Ensure the fresh air inlet to the charcoal canister is not obstructed by mud or debris.

fixSteps.title: Replace Canister Vent Valve
fixSteps.instruction: If the vent valve is faulty, replace it. This is typically a simple connector and bolt-on replacement. Clear codes and drive through a complete EVAP monitor cycle.

faq.q: Why does my fuel tank make a whooshing sound?
faq.a: The whooshing sound when you remove the gas cap is caused by excessive vacuum in the tank. This means air cannot enter the tank to replace the fuel being consumed, creating a vacuum. It is a classic symptom of P1450.

faq.q: Can fuel tank vacuum damage the tank?
faq.a: In extreme cases, yes. Prolonged excessive vacuum can deform or collapse a plastic fuel tank. More commonly, it causes fuel delivery issues and difficulty refueling.

title: Ford - EVAP Control System Canister Vent Solenoid Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: The electrical circuit for the EVAP canister vent solenoid is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
This Ford-specific code indicates an electrical fault in the EVAP canister vent solenoid circuit. Unlike P1450 which indicates a functional vacuum issue, P1451 specifically identifies an electrical problem with the vent solenoid itself, including open circuit, short circuit, or excessive resistance.

The canister vent solenoid controls the flow of fresh air into the EVAP system's charcoal canister. The PCM opens and closes this solenoid during EVAP system testing and normal operation. An electrical fault prevents the PCM from controlling the solenoid.

This code may appear alongside P1450 if the electrical failure causes the vent solenoid to remain stuck in one position.
safetyImpact: No direct safety concern. EVAP system issues affect emissions but not vehicle drivability in most cases.

causes.cause: Corroded or damaged wiring to vent solenoid
Poor connector contact at vent solenoid

fixSteps.title: Check Circuit Basics
fixSteps.instruction: Check the EVAP system fuse. Then disconnect the vent solenoid connector and measure resistance across the solenoid. Compare to Ford specifications (typically 20-40 ohms). Open or very low resistance indicates a failed solenoid.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the wiring between the PCM and the vent solenoid for damage, corrosion, or broken connections. The wiring runs under the vehicle and is exposed to road hazards.

fixSteps.title: Replace Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: If the solenoid is faulty, replace it. Clear codes and run the EVAP monitor to verify the repair.

faq.q: Is P1451 an electrical code or a performance code?
faq.a: P1451 is specifically an electrical circuit code, meaning the PCM detected a wiring or solenoid resistance issue. P1450 is the performance code that indicates the vacuum bleed-up function is not working.

faq.q: Where is the canister vent solenoid on my Ford?
faq.a: On most Ford vehicles, the canister vent solenoid is located near the charcoal canister, which is typically under the vehicle near the fuel tank area or in the rear of the vehicle.

title: Honda - EVAP Emission Control System Leak Detected (Fuel Tank System)
shortDescription: A leak has been detected in the fuel tank side of the EVAP system.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates that the EVAP system monitor has detected a leak on the fuel tank side of the evaporative emission control system. Honda's EVAP system pressurizes or applies vacuum to the fuel tank and monitors pressure decay to detect leaks.

P1456 specifically points to the fuel tank side, which includes the fuel tank itself, the fuel cap, the fuel filler neck, and the hoses connecting the tank to the EVAP canister. This distinguishes it from P1457, which points to the canister side.

The number one cause of P1456 on Honda vehicles is a loose, damaged, or worn fuel cap. Always check the fuel cap first before pursuing more complex diagnosis.
safetyImpact: No safety concern. EVAP leaks release small amounts of fuel vapor to the atmosphere but do not affect vehicle operation.

causes.cause: Loose or worn fuel cap seal
Leaking fuel tank seal or gasket
Cracked EVAP hose between tank and canister
Fuel tank rust or damage causing a small leak

symptoms: Possible faint fuel smell near the rear of the vehicle

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the fuel cap, inspect the rubber seal for cracks or deformation, and reinstall it until it clicks. If the seal is damaged, replace the fuel cap. Use a genuine Honda cap for proper fit. Clear the code and drive for several days to see if it returns.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test the Fuel Tank Side
fixSteps.instruction: If the fuel cap is not the cause, perform a smoke test on the EVAP system by introducing smoke through the EVAP service port. Inspect the fuel filler neck, tank gaskets, and EVAP hoses for smoke escaping.

fixSteps.title: Repair the Leak
fixSteps.instruction: Repair or replace the leaking component. Common repairs include replacing the filler neck, tightening tank straps, or replacing cracked EVAP hoses. Clear codes and verify the repair by running the EVAP monitor.

faq.q: Can a loose gas cap really cause a Check Engine Light?
faq.a: Yes, absolutely. A loose or improperly sealed gas cap is the single most common cause of P1456 on Honda vehicles. Always check the cap first.

faq.q: What is the difference between P1456 and P1457?
faq.a: P1456 indicates a leak on the fuel tank side of the EVAP system, while P1457 indicates a leak on the canister side. This helps narrow down the search area during diagnosis.

title: Honda - EVAP Emission Control System Leak Detected (Canister System)
shortDescription: A leak has been detected in the charcoal canister side of the EVAP system.
fullDescription:
This Honda/Acura-specific code indicates that the EVAP system has detected a leak on the canister side of the system, as opposed to the fuel tank side (P1456). The canister side includes the charcoal canister, the canister vent shut valve, the purge valve, and all the hoses connecting these components.

The charcoal canister absorbs fuel vapors from the fuel tank and releases them into the engine intake for combustion during normal driving. A leak on this side allows unprocessed fuel vapors to escape to the atmosphere.

Common Honda-specific causes include a cracked canister vent shut valve, a failed canister itself (particularly on vehicles exposed to repeated overfilling of the fuel tank), or deteriorated rubber hoses in the EVAP routing.
safetyImpact: No direct safety concern. Emissions-related only.

causes.cause: Deteriorated EVAP hoses near the canister

symptoms: Possible fuel smell near the rear of the vehicle

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP Canister Area
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the charcoal canister (usually near the rear of the vehicle or under the hood on some models). Visually inspect all hoses, connections, and the canister itself for cracks or damage.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test Canister Side
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a smoke test on the canister side of the EVAP system. Introduce smoke at the purge line and block the vent. Watch for smoke escaping from any component on the canister side.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the leaking component — commonly the canister vent shut valve or cracked hoses. If the canister itself is saturated (often from repeatedly overfilling the fuel tank), replace it. Clear codes and run the EVAP monitor.

faq.q: Can overfilling my gas tank cause P1457?
faq.a: Yes. Repeatedly topping off the fuel tank after the pump clicks off can force liquid fuel into the charcoal canister, saturating it and causing it to crack or fail. Always stop filling when the pump first clicks off.

faq.q: How do I know if my charcoal canister is bad?
faq.a: A saturated canister may feel heavier than normal, show cracks, or emit a strong fuel smell. If you can hear liquid sloshing inside when shaking it, the canister is saturated and needs replacement.

title: BMW - Intake Manifold Flap Position Error
shortDescription: The intake manifold flap (DISA valve) is not in the expected position.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates that the DME has detected a position error with the intake manifold flap, commonly known as the DISA (Differentiated Intake System Actuator) valve. The DISA valve changes the effective intake runner length to optimize airflow at different RPM ranges.

The DISA valve is known for reliability issues on BMW inline-6 engines (M52, M54, N52). The plastic flap inside the valve can break, and the actuator motor can fail. A broken flap is particularly concerning because pieces of plastic can be ingested into the engine.

When the DISA valve fails, you may hear a rattling sound from the intake manifold, especially during deceleration. This rattle is caused by the broken flap moving freely inside the manifold.
safetyImpact: A broken DISA valve flap could be ingested by the engine, potentially causing internal damage. A rattling flap should be inspected immediately.

causes.cause: Worn DISA valve pin or hinge

symptoms: Loss of low-end or mid-range torque

fixSteps.title: Listen for DISA Rattle
fixSteps.instruction: Start the engine and listen for a rattling or fluttering sound from the intake manifold area. Rev the engine and release the throttle — a broken DISA flap will rattle most noticeably during deceleration.

fixSteps.title: Inspect DISA Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the DISA valve from the intake manifold (typically 3-4 bolts). Inspect the flap for cracks or breakage. Check that the pin/hinge is intact and the flap moves freely. If the flap is broken, check inside the intake manifold for debris.

fixSteps.title: Replace DISA Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the DISA valve with a new or rebuilt unit. Upgraded aluminum-flap aftermarket DISA valves are available and more durable than the OEM plastic design. Clear codes and verify proper operation.

faq.q: Can a broken DISA flap damage my engine?
faq.a: Yes. If the plastic flap breaks off completely, pieces can be ingested into the engine through the intake valves. This can score the cylinder walls and damage the pistons. A rattling DISA valve should be addressed urgently.

faq.q: Are there upgraded DISA valves available?
faq.a: Yes, several aftermarket companies offer aluminum-flap DISA valves that are more durable than the original plastic design. These are a popular upgrade on M52 and M54 engines.

title: BMW - Throttle Valve Adaptation Spring Test Failed
shortDescription: The electronic throttle body adaptation spring test did not complete successfully.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates that the DME's test of the throttle valve return spring mechanism has failed. BMW electronic throttle bodies include a return spring that moves the throttle plate to a limp-home position (slightly open) in the event of an electronic failure. The DME periodically tests this spring mechanism.

When the test fails, it may indicate that the throttle body spring is weakened, the throttle plate is binding due to carbon buildup, or the throttle body motor is having difficulty overcoming spring resistance. The DME may enter a reduced performance mode as a precaution.

This code is commonly associated with carbon buildup on the throttle plate and bore, which is a normal accumulation on BMW engines due to crankcase ventilation oil passing through the intake.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter reduced performance mode. The throttle body spring is a safety device; if it fails, the throttle could stick in an uncontrolled position.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup on throttle plate restricting movement
Weakened or broken throttle return spring

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct and clean the throttle plate and bore with BMW-approved throttle body cleaner. Ensure the plate moves freely. Do not use excessive force on the plate.

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Using a BMW-compatible scan tool, perform the throttle body adaptation (learning) procedure. This resets the DME's knowledge of the throttle body's mechanical characteristics.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning and adaptation do not resolve the issue, the throttle body may need replacement. After installation, adaptation must be performed before the vehicle will idle correctly.

faq.q: What is the throttle adaptation spring test?
faq.a: The DME periodically tests the throttle body return spring by briefly releasing motor control and measuring how quickly the throttle plate returns to the limp-home position. This verifies the safety spring is functional.

faq.q: Do I need a special scan tool for BMW throttle adaptation?
faq.a: Yes. Standard OBD-II scanners cannot perform BMW throttle body adaptation. You need a BMW-specific tool such as ISTA, Carly, BimmerCode, or a professional diagnostic system.

title: BMW - Throttle Position Control Malfunction
shortDescription: The electronic throttle position control system is not functioning within expected parameters.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates a general malfunction in the electronic throttle position control system. The DME commands a specific throttle plate position based on accelerator pedal input, and uses the throttle position sensor feedback to verify the plate reached the correct position. When the actual position does not match the commanded position within tolerance, this code is set.

This code can be caused by a variety of issues including a dirty throttle body, a failing throttle body motor, a faulty throttle position sensor, or a wiring issue. It is one of the more common BMW driveability codes.

The DME will typically enter a reduced performance mode (similar to limp mode) when this code is set, limiting engine speed and throttle response to protect the engine and ensure driver safety.
safetyImpact: Reduced performance mode limits acceleration capability. A failing throttle body could theoretically cause unintended throttle application.

causes.cause: Dirty throttle body with carbon deposits

symptoms: Reduced engine performance or limp mode

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct and thoroughly clean the throttle body plate and bore. This is the most common fix for P1545 on BMW vehicles.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Connector and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the throttle body electrical connector for corrosion, oil contamination, or loose pins. Clean with contact cleaner and ensure a secure connection.

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Clear codes and perform throttle body adaptation using a BMW-compatible scan tool. Test drive and monitor throttle position data for proper tracking.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If the throttle body motor or sensor is faulty, replace the complete throttle body assembly. Perform adaptation after installation.

faq.q: Why does my BMW go into limp mode with P1545?
faq.a: The DME enters limp mode as a safety precaution when it cannot reliably control the throttle. This prevents unintended acceleration or throttle behavior while still allowing the vehicle to be driven at reduced speed.

faq.q: How often should I clean my BMW throttle body?
faq.a: BMW throttle bodies should be cleaned every 40,000-60,000 miles as preventive maintenance. Oil vapor from the crankcase ventilation system gradually coats the throttle plate and bore, eventually causing driveability issues.

title: BMW - Idle Air Control Valve Malfunction
shortDescription: The idle air control system is not maintaining the correct idle speed.
fullDescription:
This BMW-specific code indicates that the DME has detected a problem with the idle air control system. On older BMW models, a separate idle air control valve (IACV) controls idle speed by bypassing air around the throttle plate. On newer models with electronic throttle, the idle air control is managed by the throttle body itself.

When the idle control system malfunctions, the engine may idle too high, too low, or fluctuate (hunt). The DME monitors idle speed and compares it to the target RPM. If it cannot achieve the target after making maximum corrections, this code is set.

On BMW M52 and M54 engines, the IACV is prone to carbon buildup that restricts its air passage. On newer N-series engines, the electronic throttle body handles idle control and carbon buildup on the throttle plate can cause similar issues.
safetyImpact: Idle speed issues may cause stalling at stop lights, which could be hazardous in traffic.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup in idle air control valve or throttle body
Failed idle air control valve (older models)

symptoms: Idle too high or too low

fixSteps.title: Clean IACV or Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: On older BMWs, remove and clean the idle air control valve with throttle body cleaner. On newer models, clean the electronic throttle body. Ensure all passages are clear of carbon deposits.

fixSteps.title: Check for Vacuum Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a smoke test or visually inspect for vacuum leaks that could affect idle. Pay special attention to the CCV/PCV valve and its hoses, intake boot, and vacuum lines.

fixSteps.title: Reset Idle Adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Using a BMW-compatible scan tool, reset the idle adaptation values. Allow the engine to idle for 5 minutes without touching the throttle to allow the DME to relearn proper idle control.

faq.q: Why does my BMW idle fluctuate up and down?
faq.a: A hunting idle on BMW is commonly caused by a vacuum leak or a dirty idle air control valve/throttle body. The DME is trying to compensate for the air leak, causing the RPM to oscillate.

faq.q: Does my newer BMW have an IACV?
faq.a: Most BMW models from 2006 onwards use electronic throttle control without a separate IACV. Idle speed is controlled by the electronic throttle body itself. Cleaning the throttle body is the equivalent maintenance task.

title: Toyota - Startability Malfunction
shortDescription: The engine control module has detected an abnormal engine start condition.
fullDescription:
This Toyota/Lexus-specific code indicates that the PCM has detected an issue during the engine starting sequence. P1604 is a catch-all code that is set when the engine experiences difficulty starting, including long crank times, rough starts, or stalling immediately after starting.

This code is often stored as a history code alongside other DTCs that identify the specific root cause. By itself, P1604 does not point to a single component failure. It is Toyota's way of flagging that a problematic start event occurred.

To properly diagnose P1604, always check for additional trouble codes that were stored at the same time. The companion code will typically identify the actual failing component (fuel, ignition, sensor, etc.).
safetyImpact: Starting difficulties can be inconvenient and potentially stranding. The root cause may affect engine safety.

causes.cause: Fuel system issue (weak pump, clogged filter)
Sensor fault affecting start (CKP, CMP, MAF)
Starter motor issue causing slow cranking

fixSteps.title: Check for Companion Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all stored and pending DTCs. P1604 is usually accompanied by another code that identifies the specific problem. Diagnose and repair the companion code first.

fixSteps.title: Test Battery and Charging
fixSteps.instruction: Test battery voltage (should be 12.4V+ at rest, 13.5-14.5V running). A weak battery is the most common cause of starting problems. Load test the battery if voltage is borderline.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Delivery
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for the fuel pump priming sound when the key is turned on. Check fuel pressure if available. Verify spark is present at all cylinders during cranking.

fixSteps.title: Resolve Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Based on companion codes and testing, repair the identified fault. Clear all codes and verify the engine starts normally on multiple attempts including cold starts.

faq.q: Is P1604 a serious code?
faq.a: P1604 by itself is more of an informational flag. The severity depends on the companion code that accompanies it. Always check for additional DTCs to find the root cause.

faq.q: My Toyota shows only P1604 with no other codes. What should I do?
faq.a: If P1604 is the only code and the vehicle starts normally now, it may have been a one-time event (brief low battery, etc.). Clear the code and monitor. If it returns, check battery health and fuel system basics.

title: Nissan - Throttle Position Sensor Range/Performance
shortDescription: The throttle position sensor signal is outside the expected range on Nissan/Infiniti vehicles.
fullDescription:
This Nissan and Infiniti specific code indicates the ECM has detected an abnormal signal from the throttle position sensor (TPS) or the electronic throttle body assembly. The signal does not correlate correctly with the accelerator pedal position or expected throttle operation.

Commonly seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Sentra, Pathfinder and Infiniti G35, FX35, and QX56 models from 2002-2015. The electronic throttle body on these vehicles is known for carbon buildup and internal wear.

The vehicle may enter limp-home mode with limited throttle response. Idle learn procedure is often required after cleaning or replacing the throttle body.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode with reduced power, making highway driving and merging dangerous.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup in electronic throttle body
Wiring or connector issue at throttle body

fixSteps.title: Scan and Record Data
fixSteps.instruction: Connect scan tool and record TPS1 and TPS2 voltage at idle and WOT. Values should track smoothly.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove intake hose and clean throttle body bore and plate with throttle body cleaner. Do not scrub the coating.

fixSteps.title: Perform Idle Air Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Follow Nissan idle air volume learning procedure: warm engine, turn off accessories, then follow ignition cycle sequence with scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body If Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning and relearn fail, replace the electronic throttle body assembly and perform relearn.

faq.q: Can I just clean the throttle body to fix P1121?
faq.a: Often yes, especially on higher-mileage Nissans. Clean the throttle body and perform the idle air relearn before replacing parts.

faq.q: Do I need to relearn idle after battery disconnect?
faq.a: Yes. Nissan requires an idle air volume learn procedure after battery disconnect or throttle body service to restore proper idle.

title: Nissan - Throttle Position Sensor Low Input
shortDescription: The throttle position sensor voltage is below the expected minimum on Nissan vehicles.
fullDescription:
This Nissan specific code sets when the ECM detects a TPS signal voltage below the learned low threshold. This typically indicates a shorted sensor, open circuit, or failed throttle position sensor element.

Applies to Nissan Altima, Frontier, Xterra, Titan, and Infiniti G, FX, and QX models. Often accompanied by P1121 or P2135.

The vehicle will frequently enter limp mode limiting RPM to around 2500-3000.
safetyImpact: Severely reduced power output can make the vehicle unable to climb hills or merge safely.

causes.cause: Open or shorted TPS signal wire
Poor ground at throttle body connector

symptoms: Vehicle will not accelerate past 20-30 mph

fixSteps.title: Measure TPS Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Backprobe the TPS signal wire with key on, engine off. Compare to spec (typically 0.4-0.8V closed).

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check for broken wires, corrosion, or pushed-back pins at the throttle body connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Replace electronic throttle body assembly if voltage is out of range and wiring is good.

faq.q: Is the TPS serviceable separately on Nissan?
faq.a: On most modern Nissans the TPS is integrated into the electronic throttle body and not sold separately.

title: Nissan - Closed Throttle Position Switch Malfunction
shortDescription: The closed throttle position switch on the throttle body is not signaling correctly.
fullDescription:
Nissan sets P1126 when the closed throttle position (idle) switch signal disagrees with the throttle position sensor voltage. The ECM cannot confirm when the throttle is fully closed, which affects idle control and deceleration fuel cut.

Common on older Nissan Maxima, Pathfinder, and Frontier with mechanical throttle bodies. The switch may be contaminated or misadjusted.
safetyImpact: Poor idle quality and possible stalling at stops.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Cable
fixSteps.instruction: Verify throttle plate fully closes when pedal released. Adjust cable free-play if needed.

fixSteps.title: Test Idle Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Use multimeter to verify switch continuity changes when throttle is opened slightly.

fixSteps.title: Replace or Adjust Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Adjust or replace the closed throttle position switch per service manual.

faq.q: Can a dirty throttle body cause P1126?
faq.a: Yes, sludge can prevent the plate from closing fully and trigger this code.

title: Nissan - Intake Valve Timing Control Position Sensor
shortDescription: The intake camshaft position sensor signal is out of range on Nissan engines.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1140 indicates a problem with the intake variable valve timing (VVT) position sensor or circuit. The ECM cannot properly monitor intake camshaft position relative to the crankshaft.

Frequently seen on Nissan VQ35DE and QR25DE engines in Altima, Maxima, 350Z, and Murano. Often caused by a bad cam sensor or a stretched timing chain/tensioner issue.
safetyImpact: Possible rough running and reduced power. No immediate safety issue.

causes.cause: Stretched timing chain or worn tensioner

fixSteps.title: Check Oil Level and Condition
fixSteps.instruction: Verify oil is at correct level and not overdue for change. Dirty oil affects VVT operation.

fixSteps.title: Test Cam Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Scan live data and verify intake cam position tracks correctly. Replace sensor if erratic.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Chain
fixSteps.instruction: If rattle on cold start is present, inspect timing chain for stretch. Repair as needed.

faq.q: Will an oil change fix P1140?
faq.a: Sometimes, if dirty or low oil is causing VVT malfunction. Use correct viscosity oil and replace filter.

title: Nissan - Heated Oxygen Sensor 1 Lean Shift Monitoring
shortDescription: Bank 1 upstream oxygen sensor is indicating a lean shift outside acceptable range.
fullDescription:
This Nissan specific code is set when the front (upstream) O2 sensor on bank 1 produces voltages that skew lean during closed-loop monitoring. The sensor may be aged, contaminated, or there could be an unmetered air leak.

Common on Nissan Altima, Sentra, and Frontier with higher miles. Often appears with P0171.
safetyImpact: Long-term lean operation can damage catalyst. No immediate driveability concern.

causes.cause: Aged or contaminated upstream O2 sensor

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove MAF and spray with MAF-specific cleaner. Reinstall.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Perform smoke test of intake manifold to find vacuum leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace upstream O2 sensor on bank 1 if other checks pass.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1143?
faq.a: Short-term yes, but prolonged lean running can damage the catalytic converter.

title: Nissan - Heated Oxygen Sensor 2 Rich Shift Monitoring
shortDescription: Downstream oxygen sensor bank 1 indicates a rich shift outside acceptable range.
fullDescription:
P1146 on Nissan vehicles indicates the rear (downstream) O2 sensor on bank 1 shows voltage patterns consistent with a rich condition that does not match the front sensor.

Typical on Nissan Altima 2.5L, Sentra, and Xterra. Possible causes include a faulty sensor, exhaust leaks before the sensor, or actual rich running.
safetyImpact: Catalyst may degrade faster. No immediate safety issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for exhaust leaks between engine and sensor.

fixSteps.title: Verify Fuel Trim
fixSteps.instruction: Scan fuel trims. Address rich condition at source if trims are negative.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace bank 1 downstream O2 sensor if other causes eliminated.

faq.q: Is the downstream sensor important?
faq.a: Yes, it monitors catalyst efficiency and catches front sensor failures.

title: Nissan - Closed Loop Control Function Bank 1
shortDescription: The engine cannot maintain closed loop fuel control on bank 1.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1148 sets when the ECM cannot maintain closed-loop operation based on bank 1 O2 sensor feedback. The feedback loop is not responding as expected, keeping the system in open loop.

Common causes include a failed air-fuel ratio (A/F) sensor, wiring fault, or blown heater fuse. Frequently found on Nissan Altima, Sentra, and Frontier.
safetyImpact: Poor fuel economy and emissions. No immediate driveability concern.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect O2/AF sensor heater fuses and replace as needed.

fixSteps.title: Test A/F Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Verify A/F sensor signal and heater operation with scan tool and DMM.

fixSteps.title: Replace A/F Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace bank 1 air-fuel ratio sensor if failed.

faq.q: What is closed loop?
faq.a: Closed loop is when the ECM adjusts fuel based on O2 sensor feedback instead of a preset map.

title: Nissan - Traction Control System Signal
shortDescription: The ECM received an abnormal TCS signal from the ABS/VDC module.
fullDescription:
On Nissan vehicles, P1210 indicates the ECM detected an issue with the traction control system signal received over CAN. This affects torque reduction requests from the TCS module.

Often appears when wheel speed sensors fail or ABS module has issues. Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Pathfinder, and Infiniti G35.
safetyImpact: TCS/VDC may be disabled, reducing stability on slippery surfaces.

fixSteps.title: Scan All Modules
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for codes in ABS/VDC module. Address wheel speed sensor codes first.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wheel Speed Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Check tone rings and sensor wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Check CAN Bus
fixSteps.instruction: Verify CAN communication between ECM and ABS module.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with TCS off?
faq.a: Yes in dry conditions, but be cautious in rain or snow without traction control.

title: Nissan - Engine Over Temperature Condition
shortDescription: Engine coolant temperature has exceeded safe operating limits.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1217 indicates the ECM detected engine overheating. The ECM may implement fail-safe strategies such as disabling cylinders alternately or cutting fuel to prevent damage.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Pathfinder. Immediate attention required to prevent head gasket or engine damage.
safetyImpact: Severe overheating can destroy the engine. Stop driving immediately when light comes on.

fixSteps.title: Stop and Cool Down
fixSteps.instruction: Pull over safely, turn off engine, wait for cool down before opening cap.

fixSteps.title: Check Coolant
fixSteps.instruction: After engine is cool, check coolant level and look for leaks.

fixSteps.title: Test Cooling System
fixSteps.instruction: Pressure test cooling system, verify fan operation, test thermostat.

fixSteps.title: Repair Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed thermostat, fan, water pump, or head gasket as needed.

faq.q: Can I keep driving with P1217?
faq.a: No. Overheating can destroy the engine in minutes. Stop and address immediately.

title: Nissan - Ignition Signal Primary
shortDescription: The ECM detected no primary ignition signal from the ignition coil assemblies.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1320 is a common code indicating the ECM is not receiving the confirmation signal from one or more ignition coils. This is frequently a no-start or misfire code.

Commonly affected models include Nissan Altima, Sentra, Maxima, Frontier, Pathfinder, and Infiniti G20 with coil-on-plug ignition. Camshaft position sensor is also a very common cause.
safetyImpact: Engine may not start or may misfire severely, affecting driveability.

fixSteps.title: Check Cam/Crank Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: On older Nissans, start with camshaft and crankshaft position sensors which commonly fail.

fixSteps.title: Test Ignition Coils
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coils to isolate a failed coil. Measure primary and secondary resistance if specified.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the failed coil or sensor and clear code.

faq.q: Why does Nissan have so many P1320 cases?
faq.a: The camshaft sensors on older VQ and QG engines are prone to failure as they age.

title: Nissan - Crankshaft Position Sensor Reference
shortDescription: The ECM detected missing or invalid reference signal from the crankshaft sensor.
fullDescription:
On Nissan vehicles, P1335 indicates a crankshaft position sensor reference signal problem. The sensor either fails to generate a signal or the signal is corrupted.

Applies to Nissan Frontier, Xterra, Pathfinder, Altima, and Infiniti models with VG and VQ engines.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall or fail to start.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check sensor connector and wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace CKP Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace crankshaft position sensor.

faq.q: How can I test the CKP sensor?
faq.a: Measure resistance per service manual or check AC voltage during cranking.

title: Nissan - EGR Function Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The EGR temperature sensor or position signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1400 indicates EGR system electrical fault, typically a sensor circuit issue. The ECM cannot verify EGR valve operation via the feedback sensor.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Frontier, and Pathfinder with external EGR valves.
safetyImpact: Increased emissions. No immediate driveability problem.

causes.cause: Failed EGR temperature or position sensor

fixSteps.title: Inspect EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean EGR valve, check for stuck diaphragm.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure sensor resistance and compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component.

faq.q: Do I need to replace the whole EGR valve?
faq.a: Not always. Cleaning often restores function.

title: Nissan - EGR System Malfunction
shortDescription: The EGR system is not flowing as expected.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1402 indicates insufficient or excessive EGR flow. Typical cause is a clogged EGR passage or sticking valve.

Common on Nissan Altima 2.4L and Frontier 2.4L with older EGR systems.
safetyImpact: Possible engine knock under load; increased NOx emissions.

fixSteps.title: Clean EGR Passages
fixSteps.instruction: Remove EGR valve and clean intake passages of carbon.

fixSteps.title: Test Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Verify EGR solenoid operates when commanded with scan tool.

faq.q: Can I delete EGR?
faq.a: EGR delete is illegal for emissions-equipped vehicles and may fail inspection.

title: Nissan - EVAP System Small Leak
shortDescription: A small leak was detected in the evaporative emissions system.
fullDescription:
This Nissan code indicates the EVAP system self-test detected a small leak, typically 0.040 inch or smaller. Most common cause is a loose or damaged gas cap.

Seen on almost all late-model Nissan and Infiniti vehicles.
safetyImpact: No driveability issue. Minor increase in fuel vapor emissions.

fixSteps.title: Tighten Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and retighten gas cap until it clicks several times. Replace if damaged.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test EVAP
fixSteps.instruction: If cap is good, smoke test EVAP system to locate leak.

faq.q: Will P1440 cause me to fail smog?
faq.a: Yes, any EVAP code will typically fail emissions testing.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve
shortDescription: The EVAP vacuum cut bypass valve circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1441 indicates a problem with the vacuum cut valve (VCV) bypass valve or its control circuit in the EVAP system. This valve is used during EVAP leak testing.

Common on older Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Pathfinder.
safetyImpact: Minor emissions impact only.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Bypass Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Locate VCV bypass valve, test coil resistance.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace VCV bypass valve if failed.

faq.q: Is this expensive to fix?
faq.a: Usually no. The valve is inexpensive and access is fairly easy.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve Circuit
shortDescription: An electrical fault was detected on the EVAP vacuum cut valve circuit.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1490 indicates an open or shorted circuit to the EVAP vacuum cut valve. The ECM cannot control the valve during EVAP tests.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Frontier, Pathfinder, and Sentra.
safetyImpact: Only emissions impact.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check connector and wiring for damage or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace vacuum cut valve if internal resistance is out of spec.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1490?
faq.a: Yes, but address to pass emissions tests.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve Performance
shortDescription: The EVAP vacuum cut bypass valve is not operating mechanically as expected.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1491 indicates the vacuum cut bypass valve is not achieving the expected vacuum change when commanded. Often caused by a stuck valve or disconnected hose.

Common on Nissan Altima, Xterra, and Frontier.
safetyImpact: Emissions only.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all EVAP hoses are connected and intact.

fixSteps.title: Test Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Apply vacuum to valve with hand pump and check if it holds.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace valve if it does not hold vacuum.

faq.q: Where is the VCV located?
faq.a: Usually near the fuel tank on the EVAP canister assembly.

title: Nissan - ASCD Steering Switch Signal
shortDescription: The cruise control steering wheel switch signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1564 is set when the ASCD (cruise control) steering wheel switch signal voltage is outside expected range. Typically caused by a failed switch or clockspring issue.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Sentra, and Infiniti G.
safetyImpact: Cruise control may be disabled. No safety risk.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Measure switch resistance at each button position.

fixSteps.title: Replace Switch or Clockspring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed component per service procedure.

faq.q: Can I fix this without disabling the airbag?
faq.a: No. Safe removal of the steering wheel requires disconnecting the battery and SRS.

title: Nissan - A/T Diagnosis Communication Line
shortDescription: Communication error between ECM and TCM.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1605 indicates a communication fault between the ECM and transmission control module on the diagnosis line. Often due to wiring or a failed TCM.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Infiniti with separate TCM.
safetyImpact: Transmission may default to fail-safe mode with limited gears.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring between ECM and TCM for breaks.

fixSteps.title: Scan Both Modules
fixSteps.instruction: Scan ECM and TCM for additional codes.

faq.q: Is TCM replacement expensive?
faq.a: Yes, and it often requires programming.

title: Nissan - Park/Neutral Position Switch
shortDescription: The park/neutral switch signal is invalid.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1706 indicates the ECM cannot confirm the transmission range selector position. Often the neutral safety switch (PNP switch) has failed or is out of adjustment.

Applies to Nissan Altima, Sentra, Frontier, and Pathfinder.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start in Park or Neutral; could start in gear if switch is stuck.

fixSteps.title: Adjust or Replace PNP Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Loosen switch and adjust to align with neutral detent, or replace switch.

fixSteps.title: Verify Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring to switch with DMM.

faq.q: Why does the car not start?
faq.a: If the PNP switch fails, the starter relay may not energize even in Park.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Intermittent
shortDescription: The mass air flow sensor produced an intermittent signal.
fullDescription:
Hyundai and Kia P1100 indicates the MAF sensor signal briefly went out of range. Often caused by dirty MAF element or loose intake hose clamps.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima, Sorento.
safetyImpact: Possible hesitation or stalling.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Spray with MAF-safe cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Tighten Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Check clamps on intake hose and airbox.

faq.q: What does P1100 mean on Hyundai?
faq.a: Intermittent MAF signal fault, often cleanable.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Signal High
shortDescription: The MAF sensor signal voltage is higher than expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1103 sets when MAF signal exceeds the upper threshold, suggesting a short to voltage or actual high airflow reading.

Affects Elantra, Sonata, Accent, and Kia Rio, Soul, Optima.
safetyImpact: Possible rich running; poor fuel economy.

causes.cause: Short to voltage on signal wire

fixSteps.title: Check Signal Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure MAF signal with KOEO, should be near 0V.

fixSteps.title: Replace MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Replace MAF if reading out of spec.

faq.q: Can cleaning fix P1103?
faq.a: Sometimes. Try cleaning first; replace if code persists.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Signal Low
shortDescription: The MAF sensor signal voltage is lower than expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1104 indicates the MAF signal is at or near zero, either from a shorted sensor, broken wire, or dead sensor.

Applies to Hyundai Santa Fe, Tucson, Sonata and Kia Sportage, Optima.
safetyImpact: Lean condition possible; lack of power.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Verify MAF connector is fully seated.

fixSteps.title: Measure Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Check MAF reference voltage and ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed MAF sensor.

faq.q: Will the car run without the MAF?
faq.a: It may run poorly in limp mode using alternate fueling.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The barometric pressure sensor signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1105 indicates a problem with the BARO sensor (sometimes integrated into the MAP or MAF). Affects altitude compensation.

Affects Hyundai Accent, Elantra, Tiburon and Kia Rio.
safetyImpact: Mild driveability issues at altitude.

fixSteps.title: Scan Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Compare BARO reading to expected altitude pressure.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace BARO/MAP sensor.

faq.q: What does BARO do?
faq.a: It measures atmospheric pressure to help adjust fuel for altitude.

title: Hyundai/Kia - IAT Sensor Intermittent
shortDescription: Intermittent signal from intake air temperature sensor.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1112 indicates intermittent IAT signal issues. Can be caused by loose connector or damaged sensor element.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima.
safetyImpact: Minor driveability issue.

fixSteps.title: Reseat Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug and reseat IAT connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace IAT sensor (often integrated with MAF).

faq.q: Where is the IAT sensor?
faq.a: Usually in the intake tract or integrated into MAF.

title: Hyundai/Kia - A/F Ratio Sensor Not Switching
shortDescription: The air-fuel ratio sensor signal is not switching as expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1166 indicates the wideband A/F sensor is not responding to fuel mixture changes, typically because the sensor has aged or lost its reference.

Common on Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, Sonata and Kia Sorento.
safetyImpact: Emissions increase; possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Check Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Verify A/F sensor responds to snap throttle.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace A/F sensor if unresponsive.

faq.q: How long do A/F sensors last?
faq.a: Typically 100,000 to 150,000 miles.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Rear Heated Oxygen Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The rear O2 sensor shows abnormal behavior.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1169 indicates the downstream O2 sensor on bank 1 is not signaling correctly. May be from sensor aging or actual catalyst breakdown.

Seen across Hyundai and Kia lineup.
safetyImpact: Emissions compliance impact.
