fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for leaks upstream of sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace rear O2 sensor.

faq.q: Do I need OEM sensors?
faq.a: OEM or NTK/Denso are recommended for Hyundai/Kia.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Electronic Throttle System Malfunction
shortDescription: The electronic throttle system has detected a fault.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1295 indicates a general ETC system fault, often related to TPS correlation, motor drive, or pedal sensor.

Affects many Hyundai/Kia vehicles with drive-by-wire, especially Sonata, Elantra, Tucson, Optima, Forte.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode with severely limited throttle.

fixSteps.title: Scan Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Read TPS1, TPS2, APP1, APP2 voltages. Compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body or Pedal
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component and perform TPS relearn.

faq.q: Is this covered under warranty?
faq.a: Check your extended warranty; some Hyundai ETC issues had campaigns.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Ignition Coil C Primary Circuit
shortDescription: The ignition coil C (cylinder 3) primary circuit has an open or short.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1308 indicates the coil on cylinder 3 is not switching as commanded. Could be a failed coil or wiring fault.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima, Sorento.
safetyImpact: Misfire on cylinder 3 if coil fails; possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coil 3 with another cylinder to see if code moves.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed coil.

faq.q: Should I replace all coils at once?
faq.a: Not necessarily, but if one fails with high mileage, others may soon.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Knock Sensor Detection System (KSDS)
shortDescription: The knock sensor detection system has identified abnormal engine vibration indicating bearing wear.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1326 is a special code generated by the KSDS software update, which was applied to 2.0L and 2.4L Theta II engines. It monitors vibration patterns consistent with connecting rod bearing failure.

Affected models include 2011-2019 Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe Sport, Tucson and Kia Optima, Sorento, Sportage with Theta II GDI engines. When P1326 is set, the vehicle enters a permanent low-power mode to protect the engine and notify the owner of an impending failure. This often triggers warranty engine replacement under the Hyundai/Kia settlement program.
safetyImpact: Vehicle enters severely limited power mode to protect the engine. Sudden power loss could be dangerous in traffic.

causes.cause: Worn connecting rod bearings (Theta II defect)

fixSteps.title: Verify Warranty Coverage
fixSteps.instruction: Contact Hyundai or Kia dealer with VIN. Most P1326 cases qualify for engine replacement under extended warranty or class action settlement.

fixSteps.title: Dealer Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Dealer will perform documented inspection and submit claim.

fixSteps.title: Engine Replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Short block or complete engine replacement as approved.

faq.q: Will Hyundai cover the repair?
faq.a: Often yes. Take it to a Hyundai/Kia dealer with KSDS activated - this is the preferred evidence for warranty/settlement claims.

faq.q: Can I clear P1326?
faq.a: No, once set it is permanent and will not clear until the engine is replaced or the ECU is reflashed per the campaign.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Spark Timing Adjust Malfunction
shortDescription: The ECM cannot achieve proper spark timing.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1330 indicates ignition timing correction is outside acceptable range. May indicate a timing chain issue, bad crank/cam sensor correlation, or knock sensor fault.

Common on older Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe and Kia Sorento.
safetyImpact: Possible hesitation and reduced power.

fixSteps.title: Scan for Additional Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Check for cam/crank codes and knock sensor codes.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Chain
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for rattle; inspect for stretch.

faq.q: Is this expensive?
faq.a: Depends on root cause. Knock sensor is cheap; timing chain is expensive.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Vehicle Speed Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The vehicle speed signal is missing or erratic.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1500 indicates the ECM is not receiving a valid vehicle speed signal. Common causes include failed VSS, faulty ABS wheel sensors (on newer models that use ABS for speed), or wiring.

Seen on Hyundai Elantra, Sonata and Kia Rio, Optima.
safetyImpact: Speedometer may not work; cruise control disabled.

fixSteps.title: Scan ABS
fixSteps.instruction: Scan ABS module for wheel sensor faults first.

fixSteps.title: Replace VSS
fixSteps.instruction: Replace VSS on transmission if applicable.

faq.q: Will the car still drive?
faq.a: Yes, but speedometer and some systems may not function.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Idle Air Control Valve Circuit
shortDescription: The idle air control valve circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1505 applies to older models with an IAC valve. The circuit shows open or shorted condition.

Seen on older Hyundai Accent, Elantra, Tiburon and Kia Rio, Spectra.
safetyImpact: Rough idle or stalling.

fixSteps.title: Clean IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean IAC valve with throttle body cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Replace valve if cleaning does not resolve issue.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1505?
faq.a: Yes but stalling at idle is likely.

title: Hyundai/Kia - TCM to ECM Request MIL ON
shortDescription: The TCM has requested the ECM to illuminate the MIL.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1529 indicates the transmission control module detected an issue and requested the ECM to light the check engine lamp. The actual transmission fault code must be read from the TCM.

Affects Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe and Kia Sorento, Sportage.
safetyImpact: Possible transmission fail-safe; reduced functionality.

fixSteps.title: Scan TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Connect bidirectional scanner and read TCM codes.

fixSteps.title: Address Root Code
fixSteps.instruction: Diagnose and repair the TCM-stored fault.

faq.q: Can a generic scanner read TCM?
faq.a: Usually no. A bidirectional or OEM-level scanner is needed.

title: Hyundai/Kia - A/C Switch Signal
shortDescription: The A/C request signal to the ECM is out of range.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1540 indicates an issue with the A/C request signal or pressure switch circuit, affecting idle-up and compressor engagement.

Seen on Hyundai Accent, Elantra and Kia Rio, Spectra.
safetyImpact: A/C may not work; idle may stumble when A/C is engaged.

fixSteps.title: Check Refrigerant
fixSteps.instruction: Verify refrigerant is at spec.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Check pressure switch circuit.

faq.q: Why does idle change with A/C?
faq.a: ECM raises idle to offset compressor load.

title: Hyundai/Kia - TCM Adaptive Value Not Learned
shortDescription: The transmission adaptive shift values have not been properly learned.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1586 indicates the TCM has not completed its adaptive relearn, often after battery disconnect or TCM replacement. Drive cycle or scan tool relearn usually resolves it.

Affects Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe, Tucson and Kia Sorento, Optima with automatic transmissions.
safetyImpact: Possible harsh or delayed shifts until relearn completes.

fixSteps.title: Perform Relearn Drive Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Drive through all gears, varying throttle positions, for 15-30 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Scan Tool Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Use OEM or bidirectional scanner to initiate adaptive reset.

faq.q: Why are shifts harsh after a battery swap?
faq.a: Adaptive values were reset. They relearn as you drive.

title: Hyundai/Kia - ECM/PCM Internal Error
shortDescription: The ECM detected an internal fault.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1606 indicates an internal ECM error such as RAM/ROM failure or a corrupted calibration.

Seen across Hyundai/Kia lineup.
safetyImpact: Engine may run poorly or not start.

fixSteps.title: Check Battery and Alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Test battery voltage and alternator output. Weak electrical system can cause false triggers.

fixSteps.title: Try Reflash
fixSteps.instruction: Dealer can attempt ECM reflash before replacement.

fixSteps.title: Replace ECM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ECM if reflash fails.

faq.q: Will a new battery fix it?
faq.a: Sometimes. A failing battery can cause intermittent P1606.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Trim Bank 1 System Too Lean
shortDescription: Fuel trim on bank 1 at the lean adaptation limit at idle.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1136 indicates additive fuel adaptation at idle has reached its lean limit for bank 1. Typical on cars with vacuum leaks or failed secondary air systems.

Seen on VW Jetta, Passat, Golf and Audi A4, A6.
safetyImpact: Emissions and driveability issues.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Smoke test intake.

fixSteps.title: Repair Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Fix any leaks found.

faq.q: Is this the same as P0171?
faq.a: Very similar; P1136 is the idle-specific adaptation limit.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Trim Bank 1 Additive Mixture Rich
shortDescription: The additive fuel trim at idle for bank 1 is at its rich correction limit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1137 indicates the ECM has reduced fuel to its minimum correction and the system is still rich. May be caused by leaking injectors, high fuel pressure, or contaminated MAF.

Applies to many VW/Audi engines.
safetyImpact: Catalyst damage over time.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Test fuel pressure at spec.

fixSteps.title: Test Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Perform injector leak-down test.

faq.q: Can fuel quality cause P1137?
faq.a: Poor fuel can contribute but usually injectors or FPR are the cause.

title: VW/Audi - Long Term Fuel Trim Bank 1 Rich
shortDescription: Multiplicative fuel trim at its rich limit on bank 1.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1138 indicates the multiplicative fuel trim at partial loads is at its rich correction limit. Often paired with P1137.

Affects VW and Audi across the lineup.
safetyImpact: Fuel economy reduction, possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Check MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Clean or test MAF sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel System
fixSteps.instruction: Test fuel pressure and injector balance.

faq.q: Is this serious?
faq.a: Address soon to prevent cat damage.

title: VW/Audi - Load Calculation Cross Check Range/Performance
shortDescription: The ECM load calculation disagrees with the MAF sensor reading.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1141 indicates the calculated engine load based on RPM, throttle, and other inputs does not match the MAF reading. Typical causes include MAF malfunction, intake leak, or restricted intake.

Common on 1.8T and 2.0L engines.
safetyImpact: Reduced power, CEL.

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and replace if dirty.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Clean with MAF cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Test intake for leaks.

faq.q: Will a K&N filter cause this?
faq.a: Over-oiled K&N filters can contaminate MAF and trigger codes.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Level Too Low
shortDescription: Fuel level too low for accurate OBD monitoring.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1170 indicates the fuel level is low enough that the ECM suspends emission monitor testing. Not a fault in itself but prevents OBD readiness.

Generic across VW/Audi lineup.
safetyImpact: None.

fixSteps.title: Refuel
fixSteps.instruction: Add fuel to at least half tank.

fixSteps.title: Test Fuel Sender
fixSteps.instruction: If level gauge is wrong, test fuel level sender.

faq.q: Do I need to repair anything?
faq.a: Usually just adding fuel resolves it.

title: VW/Audi - O2 Sensor Correction Bank 1 At Limit
shortDescription: The oxygen sensor correction on bank 1 is at its adaptation limit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1176 means the ECM has reached the maximum O2 correction on bank 1 and cannot compensate further. Often accompanies P1128 or P1136.

Seen on VW and Audi 4 and 6-cylinder engines.
safetyImpact: Emissions and driveability.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose Base Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Verify fuel trims and look for vacuum leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If trims are normal, replace upstream O2 sensor.

faq.q: Should I replace both O2 sensors?
faq.a: Replace the upstream first; downstream only if showing symptoms.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Injector Cylinder 1 Circuit Short to Power
shortDescription: A short circuit to battery voltage was detected on cylinder 1 injector circuit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1213 indicates the cylinder 1 injector circuit is shorted to positive battery voltage. Often caused by wiring damage or a failed injector.

Affects VW and Audi gas and diesel engines.
safetyImpact: Misfire or non-start; possible damage to ECM driver.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check injector wiring for chafing and shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace Injector
fixSteps.instruction: Replace injector if internally shorted.

faq.q: Can a bad injector damage the ECM?
faq.a: Yes, a shorted injector can take out the driver inside the ECM.

title: VW/Audi - Sensor for Fuel Pressure Electrical Fault
shortDescription: An electrical fault was detected in the fuel pressure sensor circuit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1240 indicates an open or shorted fuel pressure sensor circuit. Primarily on FSI/TFSI direct-injection engines.

Common on Audi A3, A4, TT and VW GTI, Passat with TFSI engines.
safetyImpact: Reduced power, possible stall.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Scan live fuel pressure data. Verify sensor voltage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace fuel pressure sensor.

faq.q: Is this dangerous?
faq.a: Not dangerous but affects driveability.

title: VW/Audi - Cooling System Malfunction
shortDescription: The engine cooling system is not maintaining proper temperature.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1296 is set when the coolant temperature behavior is abnormal, often due to a stuck thermostat or failed coolant temp sensor. Very common on 2.0T engines where thermostats fail stuck open.

Affects VW Jetta, Passat, GTI and Audi A3, A4 with EA888 engines.
safetyImpact: Poor heater performance, increased emissions, possible overheating.

fixSteps.title: Check Coolant
fixSteps.instruction: Verify coolant level and condition.

fixSteps.title: Replace Thermostat
fixSteps.instruction: Replace thermostat assembly (often housing on 2.0T).

fixSteps.title: Replace Temp Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace coolant temperature sensor if code persists.

faq.q: Is the thermostat easy to replace?
faq.a: Moderate difficulty on EA888; housing is plastic and prone to cracking.

title: VW/Audi - Knock Sensor 3 Signal Low
shortDescription: Knock sensor 3 signal voltage is below range.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1328 indicates the third knock sensor (on V engines) has a low signal. Could be sensor fault or wiring.

Seen on Audi V6 and V8 engines.
safetyImpact: Ignition timing retardation for safety.

fixSteps.title: Check Torque
fixSteps.instruction: Verify sensor is torqued to spec (too loose = wrong signal).

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace knock sensor 3 if faulty.

faq.q: Do I need to remove the intake manifold?
faq.a: On many V engines, yes.

title: VW/Audi - Crankshaft/Camshaft Position Sensor Correlation
shortDescription: The camshaft and crankshaft position sensors are out of sync.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1340 indicates a mismatch between CMP and CKP signals. Likely causes include a slipped timing belt/chain, worn tensioner, or failed sensor.

Affects multiple VW/Audi engines; especially important on belt-driven engines.
safetyImpact: Risk of engine damage if timing is jumped.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing
fixSteps.instruction: Verify timing marks align. Interference engines risk damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Belt/Chain If Needed
fixSteps.instruction: Replace timing belt/chain with tensioner.

faq.q: Can this destroy my engine?
faq.a: Yes on interference engines. Do not run if timing is wrong.

title: VW/Audi - Internal Control Module Knock Control Circuit
shortDescription: An internal fault was detected in the ECM knock control circuit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1386 indicates an internal ECM issue affecting knock signal processing. May resolve with reflash or require ECM replacement.

Generic across VW/Audi.
safetyImpact: Reduced ignition advance for safety.

fixSteps.title: Check Battery/Alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Test electrical system.

fixSteps.title: Dealer Reflash
fixSteps.instruction: Have dealer check for ECM updates.

faq.q: Can I fix this without a dealer?
faq.a: Sometimes. Check battery and grounds first.

title: VW/Audi - EVAP Canister Purge Valve Circuit Short to Ground
shortDescription: The EVAP canister purge valve circuit is shorted to ground.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1471 indicates a shorted circuit on the purge valve control. The valve may be energized continuously or the solenoid may be internally shorted.

Seen across VW/Audi lineup.
safetyImpact: Emissions issue; possibly mild rough idle.

fixSteps.title: Test Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance and check for short to ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace purge valve.

faq.q: Is this urgent?
faq.a: Not urgent but fix for emissions compliance.

title: VW/Audi - Throttle Actuator Malfunction
shortDescription: The throttle actuator position is outside the expected range.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1543 indicates the electronic throttle actuator is not responding as commanded. May require throttle body adaptation (basic settings) or replacement.

Seen on VW Jetta, Passat, Golf and Audi A4, A6.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Use VCDS or similar scan tool to perform throttle body basic settings.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Replace throttle body if adaptation fails.

faq.q: Is VCDS needed?
faq.a: Most bidirectional scan tools can do the adaptation; VCDS is common for VW/Audi.

title: VW/Audi - Power Supply (B+) Terminal 30 Low Voltage
shortDescription: The ECM detected low voltage on its main power supply.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1602 is set when battery voltage at the ECM is below the operating threshold. Commonly caused by a weak battery, failed alternator, or corroded grounds.

Common on all VW/Audi models.
safetyImpact: Possible stalling, no start, or electronic module issues.

fixSteps.title: Load Test Battery
fixSteps.instruction: Have battery load tested.

fixSteps.title: Test Alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Verify alternator output (13.8-14.5V running).

fixSteps.title: Clean Grounds
fixSteps.instruction: Clean ground straps and main power lugs.

faq.q: Will a new battery clear this?
faq.a: Often yes if the battery was the root cause.

title: VW/Audi - MIL Request Signal From TCM
shortDescription: The TCM has requested MIL illumination from the ECM.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1611 indicates the TCM has requested the CEL be turned on due to a transmission-related fault. Scan the TCM to find the actual stored code.

Applies to VW/Audi with automatic transmissions.
safetyImpact: Possible transmission limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Scan TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Pull codes from TCM to find the real fault.

faq.q: Does this mean my transmission is failing?
faq.a: Not necessarily - could be a sensor or solenoid. Diagnose the TCM code.

title: Nissan - Throttle Position Sensor Range/Performance
shortDescription: The throttle position sensor signal is outside the expected range on Nissan/Infiniti vehicles.
fullDescription:
This Nissan and Infiniti specific code indicates the ECM has detected an abnormal signal from the throttle position sensor (TPS) or the electronic throttle body assembly. The signal does not correlate correctly with the accelerator pedal position or expected throttle operation.

Commonly seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Sentra, Pathfinder and Infiniti G35, FX35, and QX56 models from 2002-2015. The electronic throttle body on these vehicles is known for carbon buildup and internal wear.

The vehicle may enter limp-home mode with limited throttle response. Idle learn procedure is often required after cleaning or replacing the throttle body.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode with reduced power, making highway driving and merging dangerous.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup in electronic throttle body
Wiring or connector issue at throttle body

fixSteps.title: Scan and Record Data
fixSteps.instruction: Connect scan tool and record TPS1 and TPS2 voltage at idle and WOT. Values should track smoothly.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove intake hose and clean throttle body bore and plate with throttle body cleaner. Do not scrub the coating.

fixSteps.title: Perform Idle Air Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Follow Nissan idle air volume learning procedure: warm engine, turn off accessories, then follow ignition cycle sequence with scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body If Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning and relearn fail, replace the electronic throttle body assembly and perform relearn.

faq.q: Can I just clean the throttle body to fix P1121?
faq.a: Often yes, especially on higher-mileage Nissans. Clean the throttle body and perform the idle air relearn before replacing parts.

faq.q: Do I need to relearn idle after battery disconnect?
faq.a: Yes. Nissan requires an idle air volume learn procedure after battery disconnect or throttle body service to restore proper idle.

title: Nissan - Throttle Position Sensor Low Input
shortDescription: The throttle position sensor voltage is below the expected minimum on Nissan vehicles.
fullDescription:
This Nissan specific code sets when the ECM detects a TPS signal voltage below the learned low threshold. This typically indicates a shorted sensor, open circuit, or failed throttle position sensor element.

Applies to Nissan Altima, Frontier, Xterra, Titan, and Infiniti G, FX, and QX models. Often accompanied by P1121 or P2135.

The vehicle will frequently enter limp mode limiting RPM to around 2500-3000.
safetyImpact: Severely reduced power output can make the vehicle unable to climb hills or merge safely.

causes.cause: Open or shorted TPS signal wire
Poor ground at throttle body connector

symptoms: Vehicle will not accelerate past 20-30 mph

fixSteps.title: Measure TPS Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Backprobe the TPS signal wire with key on, engine off. Compare to spec (typically 0.4-0.8V closed).

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check for broken wires, corrosion, or pushed-back pins at the throttle body connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Replace electronic throttle body assembly if voltage is out of range and wiring is good.

faq.q: Is the TPS serviceable separately on Nissan?
faq.a: On most modern Nissans the TPS is integrated into the electronic throttle body and not sold separately.

title: Nissan - Closed Throttle Position Switch Malfunction
shortDescription: The closed throttle position switch on the throttle body is not signaling correctly.
fullDescription:
Nissan sets P1126 when the closed throttle position (idle) switch signal disagrees with the throttle position sensor voltage. The ECM cannot confirm when the throttle is fully closed, which affects idle control and deceleration fuel cut.

Common on older Nissan Maxima, Pathfinder, and Frontier with mechanical throttle bodies. The switch may be contaminated or misadjusted.
safetyImpact: Poor idle quality and possible stalling at stops.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Cable
fixSteps.instruction: Verify throttle plate fully closes when pedal released. Adjust cable free-play if needed.

fixSteps.title: Test Idle Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Use multimeter to verify switch continuity changes when throttle is opened slightly.

fixSteps.title: Replace or Adjust Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Adjust or replace the closed throttle position switch per service manual.

faq.q: Can a dirty throttle body cause P1126?
faq.a: Yes, sludge can prevent the plate from closing fully and trigger this code.

title: Nissan - Intake Valve Timing Control Position Sensor
shortDescription: The intake camshaft position sensor signal is out of range on Nissan engines.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1140 indicates a problem with the intake variable valve timing (VVT) position sensor or circuit. The ECM cannot properly monitor intake camshaft position relative to the crankshaft.

Frequently seen on Nissan VQ35DE and QR25DE engines in Altima, Maxima, 350Z, and Murano. Often caused by a bad cam sensor or a stretched timing chain/tensioner issue.
safetyImpact: Possible rough running and reduced power. No immediate safety issue.

causes.cause: Stretched timing chain or worn tensioner

fixSteps.title: Check Oil Level and Condition
fixSteps.instruction: Verify oil is at correct level and not overdue for change. Dirty oil affects VVT operation.

fixSteps.title: Test Cam Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Scan live data and verify intake cam position tracks correctly. Replace sensor if erratic.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Chain
fixSteps.instruction: If rattle on cold start is present, inspect timing chain for stretch. Repair as needed.

faq.q: Will an oil change fix P1140?
faq.a: Sometimes, if dirty or low oil is causing VVT malfunction. Use correct viscosity oil and replace filter.

title: Nissan - Heated Oxygen Sensor 1 Lean Shift Monitoring
shortDescription: Bank 1 upstream oxygen sensor is indicating a lean shift outside acceptable range.
fullDescription:
This Nissan specific code is set when the front (upstream) O2 sensor on bank 1 produces voltages that skew lean during closed-loop monitoring. The sensor may be aged, contaminated, or there could be an unmetered air leak.

Common on Nissan Altima, Sentra, and Frontier with higher miles. Often appears with P0171.
safetyImpact: Long-term lean operation can damage catalyst. No immediate driveability concern.

causes.cause: Aged or contaminated upstream O2 sensor

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove MAF and spray with MAF-specific cleaner. Reinstall.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Perform smoke test of intake manifold to find vacuum leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace upstream O2 sensor on bank 1 if other checks pass.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1143?
faq.a: Short-term yes, but prolonged lean running can damage the catalytic converter.

title: Nissan - Heated Oxygen Sensor 2 Rich Shift Monitoring
shortDescription: Downstream oxygen sensor bank 1 indicates a rich shift outside acceptable range.
fullDescription:
P1146 on Nissan vehicles indicates the rear (downstream) O2 sensor on bank 1 shows voltage patterns consistent with a rich condition that does not match the front sensor.

Typical on Nissan Altima 2.5L, Sentra, and Xterra. Possible causes include a faulty sensor, exhaust leaks before the sensor, or actual rich running.
safetyImpact: Catalyst may degrade faster. No immediate safety issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for exhaust leaks between engine and sensor.

fixSteps.title: Verify Fuel Trim
fixSteps.instruction: Scan fuel trims. Address rich condition at source if trims are negative.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace bank 1 downstream O2 sensor if other causes eliminated.

faq.q: Is the downstream sensor important?
faq.a: Yes, it monitors catalyst efficiency and catches front sensor failures.

title: Nissan - Closed Loop Control Function Bank 1
shortDescription: The engine cannot maintain closed loop fuel control on bank 1.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1148 sets when the ECM cannot maintain closed-loop operation based on bank 1 O2 sensor feedback. The feedback loop is not responding as expected, keeping the system in open loop.

Common causes include a failed air-fuel ratio (A/F) sensor, wiring fault, or blown heater fuse. Frequently found on Nissan Altima, Sentra, and Frontier.
safetyImpact: Poor fuel economy and emissions. No immediate driveability concern.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect O2/AF sensor heater fuses and replace as needed.

fixSteps.title: Test A/F Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Verify A/F sensor signal and heater operation with scan tool and DMM.

fixSteps.title: Replace A/F Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace bank 1 air-fuel ratio sensor if failed.

faq.q: What is closed loop?
faq.a: Closed loop is when the ECM adjusts fuel based on O2 sensor feedback instead of a preset map.

title: Nissan - Traction Control System Signal
shortDescription: The ECM received an abnormal TCS signal from the ABS/VDC module.
fullDescription:
On Nissan vehicles, P1210 indicates the ECM detected an issue with the traction control system signal received over CAN. This affects torque reduction requests from the TCS module.

Often appears when wheel speed sensors fail or ABS module has issues. Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Pathfinder, and Infiniti G35.
safetyImpact: TCS/VDC may be disabled, reducing stability on slippery surfaces.

fixSteps.title: Scan All Modules
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for codes in ABS/VDC module. Address wheel speed sensor codes first.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wheel Speed Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Check tone rings and sensor wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Check CAN Bus
fixSteps.instruction: Verify CAN communication between ECM and ABS module.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with TCS off?
faq.a: Yes in dry conditions, but be cautious in rain or snow without traction control.

title: Nissan - Engine Over Temperature Condition
shortDescription: Engine coolant temperature has exceeded safe operating limits.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1217 indicates the ECM detected engine overheating. The ECM may implement fail-safe strategies such as disabling cylinders alternately or cutting fuel to prevent damage.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Pathfinder. Immediate attention required to prevent head gasket or engine damage.
safetyImpact: Severe overheating can destroy the engine. Stop driving immediately when light comes on.

fixSteps.title: Stop and Cool Down
fixSteps.instruction: Pull over safely, turn off engine, wait for cool down before opening cap.

fixSteps.title: Check Coolant
fixSteps.instruction: After engine is cool, check coolant level and look for leaks.

fixSteps.title: Test Cooling System
fixSteps.instruction: Pressure test cooling system, verify fan operation, test thermostat.

fixSteps.title: Repair Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed thermostat, fan, water pump, or head gasket as needed.

faq.q: Can I keep driving with P1217?
faq.a: No. Overheating can destroy the engine in minutes. Stop and address immediately.

title: Nissan - Ignition Signal Primary
shortDescription: The ECM detected no primary ignition signal from the ignition coil assemblies.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1320 is a common code indicating the ECM is not receiving the confirmation signal from one or more ignition coils. This is frequently a no-start or misfire code.

Commonly affected models include Nissan Altima, Sentra, Maxima, Frontier, Pathfinder, and Infiniti G20 with coil-on-plug ignition. Camshaft position sensor is also a very common cause.
safetyImpact: Engine may not start or may misfire severely, affecting driveability.

fixSteps.title: Check Cam/Crank Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: On older Nissans, start with camshaft and crankshaft position sensors which commonly fail.

fixSteps.title: Test Ignition Coils
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coils to isolate a failed coil. Measure primary and secondary resistance if specified.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the failed coil or sensor and clear code.

faq.q: Why does Nissan have so many P1320 cases?
faq.a: The camshaft sensors on older VQ and QG engines are prone to failure as they age.

title: Nissan - Crankshaft Position Sensor Reference
shortDescription: The ECM detected missing or invalid reference signal from the crankshaft sensor.
fullDescription:
On Nissan vehicles, P1335 indicates a crankshaft position sensor reference signal problem. The sensor either fails to generate a signal or the signal is corrupted.

Applies to Nissan Frontier, Xterra, Pathfinder, Altima, and Infiniti models with VG and VQ engines.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall or fail to start.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check sensor connector and wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace CKP Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace crankshaft position sensor.

faq.q: How can I test the CKP sensor?
faq.a: Measure resistance per service manual or check AC voltage during cranking.

title: Nissan - EGR Function Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The EGR temperature sensor or position signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1400 indicates EGR system electrical fault, typically a sensor circuit issue. The ECM cannot verify EGR valve operation via the feedback sensor.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Frontier, and Pathfinder with external EGR valves.
safetyImpact: Increased emissions. No immediate driveability problem.

causes.cause: Failed EGR temperature or position sensor

fixSteps.title: Inspect EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean EGR valve, check for stuck diaphragm.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure sensor resistance and compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component.

faq.q: Do I need to replace the whole EGR valve?
faq.a: Not always. Cleaning often restores function.

title: Nissan - EGR System Malfunction
shortDescription: The EGR system is not flowing as expected.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1402 indicates insufficient or excessive EGR flow. Typical cause is a clogged EGR passage or sticking valve.

Common on Nissan Altima 2.4L and Frontier 2.4L with older EGR systems.
safetyImpact: Possible engine knock under load; increased NOx emissions.

fixSteps.title: Clean EGR Passages
fixSteps.instruction: Remove EGR valve and clean intake passages of carbon.

fixSteps.title: Test Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Verify EGR solenoid operates when commanded with scan tool.

faq.q: Can I delete EGR?
faq.a: EGR delete is illegal for emissions-equipped vehicles and may fail inspection.

title: Nissan - EVAP System Small Leak
shortDescription: A small leak was detected in the evaporative emissions system.
fullDescription:
This Nissan code indicates the EVAP system self-test detected a small leak, typically 0.040 inch or smaller. Most common cause is a loose or damaged gas cap.

Seen on almost all late-model Nissan and Infiniti vehicles.
safetyImpact: No driveability issue. Minor increase in fuel vapor emissions.

fixSteps.title: Tighten Gas Cap
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and retighten gas cap until it clicks several times. Replace if damaged.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test EVAP
fixSteps.instruction: If cap is good, smoke test EVAP system to locate leak.

faq.q: Will P1440 cause me to fail smog?
faq.a: Yes, any EVAP code will typically fail emissions testing.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve
shortDescription: The EVAP vacuum cut bypass valve circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1441 indicates a problem with the vacuum cut valve (VCV) bypass valve or its control circuit in the EVAP system. This valve is used during EVAP leak testing.

Common on older Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Pathfinder.
safetyImpact: Minor emissions impact only.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Bypass Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Locate VCV bypass valve, test coil resistance.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace VCV bypass valve if failed.

faq.q: Is this expensive to fix?
faq.a: Usually no. The valve is inexpensive and access is fairly easy.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve Circuit
shortDescription: An electrical fault was detected on the EVAP vacuum cut valve circuit.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1490 indicates an open or shorted circuit to the EVAP vacuum cut valve. The ECM cannot control the valve during EVAP tests.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Frontier, Pathfinder, and Sentra.
safetyImpact: Only emissions impact.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check connector and wiring for damage or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace vacuum cut valve if internal resistance is out of spec.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1490?
faq.a: Yes, but address to pass emissions tests.

title: Nissan - Vacuum Cut Valve Bypass Valve Performance
shortDescription: The EVAP vacuum cut bypass valve is not operating mechanically as expected.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1491 indicates the vacuum cut bypass valve is not achieving the expected vacuum change when commanded. Often caused by a stuck valve or disconnected hose.

Common on Nissan Altima, Xterra, and Frontier.
safetyImpact: Emissions only.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Verify all EVAP hoses are connected and intact.

fixSteps.title: Test Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Apply vacuum to valve with hand pump and check if it holds.

fixSteps.title: Replace Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace valve if it does not hold vacuum.

faq.q: Where is the VCV located?
faq.a: Usually near the fuel tank on the EVAP canister assembly.

title: Nissan - ASCD Steering Switch Signal
shortDescription: The cruise control steering wheel switch signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1564 is set when the ASCD (cruise control) steering wheel switch signal voltage is outside expected range. Typically caused by a failed switch or clockspring issue.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, Sentra, and Infiniti G.
safetyImpact: Cruise control may be disabled. No safety risk.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Measure switch resistance at each button position.

fixSteps.title: Replace Switch or Clockspring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed component per service procedure.

faq.q: Can I fix this without disabling the airbag?
faq.a: No. Safe removal of the steering wheel requires disconnecting the battery and SRS.

title: Nissan - A/T Diagnosis Communication Line
shortDescription: Communication error between ECM and TCM.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1605 indicates a communication fault between the ECM and transmission control module on the diagnosis line. Often due to wiring or a failed TCM.

Seen on Nissan Altima, Maxima, and Infiniti with separate TCM.
safetyImpact: Transmission may default to fail-safe mode with limited gears.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring between ECM and TCM for breaks.

fixSteps.title: Scan Both Modules
fixSteps.instruction: Scan ECM and TCM for additional codes.

faq.q: Is TCM replacement expensive?
faq.a: Yes, and it often requires programming.

title: Nissan - Park/Neutral Position Switch
shortDescription: The park/neutral switch signal is invalid.
fullDescription:
Nissan P1706 indicates the ECM cannot confirm the transmission range selector position. Often the neutral safety switch (PNP switch) has failed or is out of adjustment.

Applies to Nissan Altima, Sentra, Frontier, and Pathfinder.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start in Park or Neutral; could start in gear if switch is stuck.

fixSteps.title: Adjust or Replace PNP Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Loosen switch and adjust to align with neutral detent, or replace switch.

fixSteps.title: Verify Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring to switch with DMM.

faq.q: Why does the car not start?
faq.a: If the PNP switch fails, the starter relay may not energize even in Park.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Intermittent
shortDescription: The mass air flow sensor produced an intermittent signal.
fullDescription:
Hyundai and Kia P1100 indicates the MAF sensor signal briefly went out of range. Often caused by dirty MAF element or loose intake hose clamps.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima, Sorento.
safetyImpact: Possible hesitation or stalling.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Spray with MAF-safe cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Tighten Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Check clamps on intake hose and airbox.

faq.q: What does P1100 mean on Hyundai?
faq.a: Intermittent MAF signal fault, often cleanable.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Signal High
shortDescription: The MAF sensor signal voltage is higher than expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1103 sets when MAF signal exceeds the upper threshold, suggesting a short to voltage or actual high airflow reading.

Affects Elantra, Sonata, Accent, and Kia Rio, Soul, Optima.
safetyImpact: Possible rich running; poor fuel economy.

causes.cause: Short to voltage on signal wire

fixSteps.title: Check Signal Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure MAF signal with KOEO, should be near 0V.

fixSteps.title: Replace MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Replace MAF if reading out of spec.

faq.q: Can cleaning fix P1103?
faq.a: Sometimes. Try cleaning first; replace if code persists.

title: Hyundai/Kia - MAF Sensor Signal Low
shortDescription: The MAF sensor signal voltage is lower than expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1104 indicates the MAF signal is at or near zero, either from a shorted sensor, broken wire, or dead sensor.

Applies to Hyundai Santa Fe, Tucson, Sonata and Kia Sportage, Optima.
safetyImpact: Lean condition possible; lack of power.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Verify MAF connector is fully seated.

fixSteps.title: Measure Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Check MAF reference voltage and ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed MAF sensor.

faq.q: Will the car run without the MAF?
faq.a: It may run poorly in limp mode using alternate fueling.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The barometric pressure sensor signal is out of range.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1105 indicates a problem with the BARO sensor (sometimes integrated into the MAP or MAF). Affects altitude compensation.

Affects Hyundai Accent, Elantra, Tiburon and Kia Rio.
safetyImpact: Mild driveability issues at altitude.

fixSteps.title: Scan Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Compare BARO reading to expected altitude pressure.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace BARO/MAP sensor.

faq.q: What does BARO do?
faq.a: It measures atmospheric pressure to help adjust fuel for altitude.

title: Hyundai/Kia - IAT Sensor Intermittent
shortDescription: Intermittent signal from intake air temperature sensor.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1112 indicates intermittent IAT signal issues. Can be caused by loose connector or damaged sensor element.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima.
safetyImpact: Minor driveability issue.

fixSteps.title: Reseat Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug and reseat IAT connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace IAT sensor (often integrated with MAF).

faq.q: Where is the IAT sensor?
faq.a: Usually in the intake tract or integrated into MAF.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Fuel System Control Closed Loop Bank 1
shortDescription: The fuel trim at closed loop is at its limit on bank 1.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1130 indicates fuel trim is at its correction limit, typically from a vacuum leak, fuel pressure problem, or O2 sensor issue.

Affects Hyundai Sonata, Tucson, Santa Fe and Kia Optima, Sorento.
safetyImpact: Long-term lean or rich can damage catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Trims
fixSteps.instruction: Read short and long-term fuel trims to determine direction.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Smoke test intake for vacuum leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace upstream O2 if aged.

faq.q: Is P1130 serious?
faq.a: Address soon to avoid catalyst damage.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Heated Oxygen Sensor Heater Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1
shortDescription: The upstream O2 sensor heater circuit has a fault.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1135 indicates the heater element or circuit in the bank 1 upstream O2 sensor is not operating correctly. Often the sensor has aged.

Seen on most Hyundai and Kia models.
safetyImpact: Delayed closed-loop operation; minor emissions impact.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Verify O2 sensor heater fuse.

fixSteps.title: Test Heater
fixSteps.instruction: Measure heater resistance per spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sensor if heater failed.

faq.q: Is this the same as P0135?
faq.a: Yes, nearly identical; P1135 is manufacturer-specific extension.

title: Hyundai/Kia - A/F Ratio Sensor Not Switching
shortDescription: The air-fuel ratio sensor signal is not switching as expected.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1166 indicates the wideband A/F sensor is not responding to fuel mixture changes, typically because the sensor has aged or lost its reference.

Common on Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, Sonata and Kia Sorento.
safetyImpact: Emissions increase; possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Check Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Verify A/F sensor responds to snap throttle.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace A/F sensor if unresponsive.

faq.q: How long do A/F sensors last?
faq.a: Typically 100,000 to 150,000 miles.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Rear Heated Oxygen Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The rear O2 sensor shows abnormal behavior.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1169 indicates the downstream O2 sensor on bank 1 is not signaling correctly. May be from sensor aging or actual catalyst breakdown.

Seen across Hyundai and Kia lineup.
safetyImpact: Emissions compliance impact.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for leaks upstream of sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace rear O2 sensor.

faq.q: Do I need OEM sensors?
faq.a: OEM or NTK/Denso are recommended for Hyundai/Kia.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Electronic Throttle System Malfunction
shortDescription: The electronic throttle system has detected a fault.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1295 indicates a general ETC system fault, often related to TPS correlation, motor drive, or pedal sensor.

Affects many Hyundai/Kia vehicles with drive-by-wire, especially Sonata, Elantra, Tucson, Optima, Forte.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode with severely limited throttle.

fixSteps.title: Scan Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Read TPS1, TPS2, APP1, APP2 voltages. Compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body or Pedal
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component and perform TPS relearn.

faq.q: Is this covered under warranty?
faq.a: Check your extended warranty; some Hyundai ETC issues had campaigns.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Ignition Coil C Primary Circuit
shortDescription: The ignition coil C (cylinder 3) primary circuit has an open or short.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1308 indicates the coil on cylinder 3 is not switching as commanded. Could be a failed coil or wiring fault.

Seen on Hyundai Sonata, Elantra, Santa Fe and Kia Optima, Sorento.
safetyImpact: Misfire on cylinder 3 if coil fails; possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Swap Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap coil 3 with another cylinder to see if code moves.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed coil.

faq.q: Should I replace all coils at once?
faq.a: Not necessarily, but if one fails with high mileage, others may soon.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Knock Sensor Detection System (KSDS)
shortDescription: The knock sensor detection system has identified abnormal engine vibration indicating bearing wear.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1326 is a special code generated by the KSDS software update, which was applied to 2.0L and 2.4L Theta II engines. It monitors vibration patterns consistent with connecting rod bearing failure.

Affected models include 2011-2019 Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe Sport, Tucson and Kia Optima, Sorento, Sportage with Theta II GDI engines. When P1326 is set, the vehicle enters a permanent low-power mode to protect the engine and notify the owner of an impending failure. This often triggers warranty engine replacement under the Hyundai/Kia settlement program.
safetyImpact: Vehicle enters severely limited power mode to protect the engine. Sudden power loss could be dangerous in traffic.

causes.cause: Worn connecting rod bearings (Theta II defect)

fixSteps.title: Verify Warranty Coverage
fixSteps.instruction: Contact Hyundai or Kia dealer with VIN. Most P1326 cases qualify for engine replacement under extended warranty or class action settlement.

fixSteps.title: Dealer Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Dealer will perform documented inspection and submit claim.

fixSteps.title: Engine Replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Short block or complete engine replacement as approved.

faq.q: Will Hyundai cover the repair?
faq.a: Often yes. Take it to a Hyundai/Kia dealer with KSDS activated - this is the preferred evidence for warranty/settlement claims.

faq.q: Can I clear P1326?
faq.a: No, once set it is permanent and will not clear until the engine is replaced or the ECU is reflashed per the campaign.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Spark Timing Adjust Malfunction
shortDescription: The ECM cannot achieve proper spark timing.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1330 indicates ignition timing correction is outside acceptable range. May indicate a timing chain issue, bad crank/cam sensor correlation, or knock sensor fault.

Common on older Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe and Kia Sorento.
safetyImpact: Possible hesitation and reduced power.

fixSteps.title: Scan for Additional Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Check for cam/crank codes and knock sensor codes.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Timing Chain
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for rattle; inspect for stretch.

faq.q: Is this expensive?
faq.a: Depends on root cause. Knock sensor is cheap; timing chain is expensive.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Vehicle Speed Sensor Malfunction
shortDescription: The vehicle speed signal is missing or erratic.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1500 indicates the ECM is not receiving a valid vehicle speed signal. Common causes include failed VSS, faulty ABS wheel sensors (on newer models that use ABS for speed), or wiring.

Seen on Hyundai Elantra, Sonata and Kia Rio, Optima.
safetyImpact: Speedometer may not work; cruise control disabled.

fixSteps.title: Scan ABS
fixSteps.instruction: Scan ABS module for wheel sensor faults first.

fixSteps.title: Replace VSS
fixSteps.instruction: Replace VSS on transmission if applicable.

faq.q: Will the car still drive?
faq.a: Yes, but speedometer and some systems may not function.

title: Hyundai/Kia - Idle Air Control Valve Circuit
shortDescription: The idle air control valve circuit is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1505 applies to older models with an IAC valve. The circuit shows open or shorted condition.

Seen on older Hyundai Accent, Elantra, Tiburon and Kia Rio, Spectra.
safetyImpact: Rough idle or stalling.

fixSteps.title: Clean IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean IAC valve with throttle body cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Replace valve if cleaning does not resolve issue.

faq.q: Can I drive with P1505?
faq.a: Yes but stalling at idle is likely.

title: Hyundai/Kia - TCM to ECM Request MIL ON
shortDescription: The TCM has requested the ECM to illuminate the MIL.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1529 indicates the transmission control module detected an issue and requested the ECM to light the check engine lamp. The actual transmission fault code must be read from the TCM.

Affects Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe and Kia Sorento, Sportage.
safetyImpact: Possible transmission fail-safe; reduced functionality.

fixSteps.title: Scan TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Connect bidirectional scanner and read TCM codes.

fixSteps.title: Address Root Code
fixSteps.instruction: Diagnose and repair the TCM-stored fault.

faq.q: Can a generic scanner read TCM?
faq.a: Usually no. A bidirectional or OEM-level scanner is needed.

title: Hyundai/Kia - A/C Switch Signal
shortDescription: The A/C request signal to the ECM is out of range.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1540 indicates an issue with the A/C request signal or pressure switch circuit, affecting idle-up and compressor engagement.

Seen on Hyundai Accent, Elantra and Kia Rio, Spectra.
safetyImpact: A/C may not work; idle may stumble when A/C is engaged.

fixSteps.title: Check Refrigerant
fixSteps.instruction: Verify refrigerant is at spec.

fixSteps.title: Test Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Check pressure switch circuit.

faq.q: Why does idle change with A/C?
faq.a: ECM raises idle to offset compressor load.

title: Hyundai/Kia - TCM Adaptive Value Not Learned
shortDescription: The transmission adaptive shift values have not been properly learned.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1586 indicates the TCM has not completed its adaptive relearn, often after battery disconnect or TCM replacement. Drive cycle or scan tool relearn usually resolves it.

Affects Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe, Tucson and Kia Sorento, Optima with automatic transmissions.
safetyImpact: Possible harsh or delayed shifts until relearn completes.

fixSteps.title: Perform Relearn Drive Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Drive through all gears, varying throttle positions, for 15-30 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Scan Tool Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Use OEM or bidirectional scanner to initiate adaptive reset.

faq.q: Why are shifts harsh after a battery swap?
faq.a: Adaptive values were reset. They relearn as you drive.

title: Hyundai/Kia - ECM/PCM Internal Error
shortDescription: The ECM detected an internal fault.
fullDescription:
Hyundai/Kia P1606 indicates an internal ECM error such as RAM/ROM failure or a corrupted calibration.

Seen across Hyundai/Kia lineup.
safetyImpact: Engine may run poorly or not start.

fixSteps.title: Check Battery and Alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Test battery voltage and alternator output. Weak electrical system can cause false triggers.

fixSteps.title: Try Reflash
fixSteps.instruction: Dealer can attempt ECM reflash before replacement.

fixSteps.title: Replace ECM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ECM if reflash fails.

faq.q: Will a new battery fix it?
faq.a: Sometimes. A failing battery can cause intermittent P1606.

title: VW/Audi - Long Term Fuel Trim Mixture Adaptation Bank 1 Lean
shortDescription: The long-term fuel trim for bank 1 is at its maximum lean correction.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1128 sets when the ECM has added maximum fuel correction to bank 1 and still cannot reach stoichiometric. Frequently caused by intake vacuum leaks (PCV, intake boot cracks) common on 1.8T, 2.0T, and older VR6 engines.

Affects VW Jetta, Golf, Passat, Beetle and Audi A4, A6, TT with 1.8T, 2.0T, and similar engines.
safetyImpact: Possible hesitation, long-term catalyst damage if unresolved.

causes.cause: Intake manifold gasket or boot leak
Weak fuel pump or clogged filter

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCV System
fixSteps.instruction: On 2.0T engines, inspect PCV valve cover or diaphragm for leaks. Very common failure point.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test Intake
fixSteps.instruction: Smoke test the intake system to locate leaks.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the MAF sensor with MAF-specific cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Repair Leak
fixSteps.instruction: Replace PCV, intake boot, or valve cover as needed.

faq.q: What is the most common cause of P1128 on a 2.0T?
faq.a: PCV diaphragm failure in the valve cover. Inspecting and replacing it is the first thing to try.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Trim Bank 1 System Too Lean
shortDescription: Fuel trim on bank 1 at the lean adaptation limit at idle.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1136 indicates additive fuel adaptation at idle has reached its lean limit for bank 1. Typical on cars with vacuum leaks or failed secondary air systems.

Seen on VW Jetta, Passat, Golf and Audi A4, A6.
safetyImpact: Emissions and driveability issues.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Smoke test intake.

fixSteps.title: Repair Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Fix any leaks found.

faq.q: Is this the same as P0171?
faq.a: Very similar; P1136 is the idle-specific adaptation limit.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Trim Bank 1 Additive Mixture Rich
shortDescription: The additive fuel trim at idle for bank 1 is at its rich correction limit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1137 indicates the ECM has reduced fuel to its minimum correction and the system is still rich. May be caused by leaking injectors, high fuel pressure, or contaminated MAF.

Applies to many VW/Audi engines.
safetyImpact: Catalyst damage over time.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Test fuel pressure at spec.

fixSteps.title: Test Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Perform injector leak-down test.

faq.q: Can fuel quality cause P1137?
faq.a: Poor fuel can contribute but usually injectors or FPR are the cause.

title: VW/Audi - Long Term Fuel Trim Bank 1 Rich
shortDescription: Multiplicative fuel trim at its rich limit on bank 1.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1138 indicates the multiplicative fuel trim at partial loads is at its rich correction limit. Often paired with P1137.

Affects VW and Audi across the lineup.
safetyImpact: Fuel economy reduction, possible catalyst damage.

fixSteps.title: Check MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Clean or test MAF sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel System
fixSteps.instruction: Test fuel pressure and injector balance.

faq.q: Is this serious?
faq.a: Address soon to prevent cat damage.

title: VW/Audi - Load Calculation Cross Check Range/Performance
shortDescription: The ECM load calculation disagrees with the MAF sensor reading.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1141 indicates the calculated engine load based on RPM, throttle, and other inputs does not match the MAF reading. Typical causes include MAF malfunction, intake leak, or restricted intake.

Common on 1.8T and 2.0L engines.
safetyImpact: Reduced power, CEL.

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect and replace if dirty.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF
fixSteps.instruction: Clean with MAF cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Test intake for leaks.

faq.q: Will a K&N filter cause this?
faq.a: Over-oiled K&N filters can contaminate MAF and trigger codes.

title: VW/Audi - O2 Control Bank 2 At Lean Limit
shortDescription: Bank 2 O2 control is stuck at lean adaptation limit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1150 indicates the long-term oxygen sensor fuel adaptation on bank 2 has reached its lean limit. V6 and V8 engines often see this from bank-specific intake leaks.

Seen on Audi A4, A6, A8 V6 and V8 engines.
safetyImpact: Driveability and emissions impact.

fixSteps.title: Identify Bank 2
fixSteps.instruction: Bank 2 contains cylinder 2 on VW/Audi V engines - verify your model.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Smoke test intake on bank 2 side.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 if Needed
fixSteps.instruction: Replace bank 2 upstream O2 sensor.

faq.q: Which bank is bank 2 on Audi?
faq.a: Usually the bank containing cylinder 2, but verify for your specific engine.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Level Too Low
shortDescription: Fuel level too low for accurate OBD monitoring.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1170 indicates the fuel level is low enough that the ECM suspends emission monitor testing. Not a fault in itself but prevents OBD readiness.

Generic across VW/Audi lineup.
safetyImpact: None.

fixSteps.title: Refuel
fixSteps.instruction: Add fuel to at least half tank.

fixSteps.title: Test Fuel Sender
fixSteps.instruction: If level gauge is wrong, test fuel level sender.

faq.q: Do I need to repair anything?
faq.a: Usually just adding fuel resolves it.

title: VW/Audi - O2 Sensor Correction Bank 1 At Limit
shortDescription: The oxygen sensor correction on bank 1 is at its adaptation limit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1176 means the ECM has reached the maximum O2 correction on bank 1 and cannot compensate further. Often accompanies P1128 or P1136.

Seen on VW and Audi 4 and 6-cylinder engines.
safetyImpact: Emissions and driveability.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose Base Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Verify fuel trims and look for vacuum leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If trims are normal, replace upstream O2 sensor.

faq.q: Should I replace both O2 sensors?
faq.a: Replace the upstream first; downstream only if showing symptoms.

title: VW/Audi - Fuel Injector Cylinder 1 Circuit Short to Power
shortDescription: A short circuit to battery voltage was detected on cylinder 1 injector circuit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1213 indicates the cylinder 1 injector circuit is shorted to positive battery voltage. Often caused by wiring damage or a failed injector.

Affects VW and Audi gas and diesel engines.
safetyImpact: Misfire or non-start; possible damage to ECM driver.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check injector wiring for chafing and shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace Injector
fixSteps.instruction: Replace injector if internally shorted.

faq.q: Can a bad injector damage the ECM?
faq.a: Yes, a shorted injector can take out the driver inside the ECM.

title: VW/Audi - Sensor for Fuel Pressure Electrical Fault
shortDescription: An electrical fault was detected in the fuel pressure sensor circuit.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1240 indicates an open or shorted fuel pressure sensor circuit. Primarily on FSI/TFSI direct-injection engines.

Common on Audi A3, A4, TT and VW GTI, Passat with TFSI engines.
safetyImpact: Reduced power, possible stall.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Scan live fuel pressure data. Verify sensor voltage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace fuel pressure sensor.

faq.q: Is this dangerous?
faq.a: Not dangerous but affects driveability.

title: VW/Audi - Cooling System Malfunction
shortDescription: The engine cooling system is not maintaining proper temperature.
fullDescription:
VW/Audi P1296 is set when the coolant temperature behavior is abnormal, often due to a stuck thermostat or failed coolant temp sensor. Very common on 2.0T engines where thermostats fail stuck open.

Affects VW Jetta, Passat, GTI and Audi A3, A4 with EA888 engines.
safetyImpact: Poor heater performance, increased emissions, possible overheating.

fixSteps.title: Check Coolant
fixSteps.instruction: Verify coolant level and condition.

fixSteps.title: Replace Thermostat
fixSteps.instruction: Replace thermostat assembly (often housing on 2.0T).

fixSteps.title: Replace Temp Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace coolant temperature sensor if code persists.

faq.q: Is the thermostat easy to replace?
faq.a: Moderate difficulty on EA888; housing is plastic and prone to cracking.

title: VW/Audi - Knock Sensor 3 Signal Low
shortDescription: Knock sensor 3 signal voltage is below range.
fullDescription:

title: Tesla - High Voltage Battery Cell Imbalance

fullDescription:

Over time, individual cells age at different rates. The BMS normally balances them during charging, but if imbalance exceeds thresholds, this code sets. Coverage includes all Tesla vehicles 2012+.
safetyImpact: Reduced range, limited regenerative braking, potential further battery damage if ignored.

fixSteps.title: Scan with Tesla Toolbox
fixSteps.instruction: Use Tesla diagnostic tool to identify imbalanced modules.

fixSteps.title: Perform balance charge

fixSteps.title: Module replacement
fixSteps.instruction: If imbalance persists, service center must replace the affected battery module.

faq.q: Is my battery dying?
faq.a: Not necessarily - minor imbalance is normal. Persistent P1A00 indicates a weak module.

The vehicle may limit power or refuse to drive. Coverage: Model S, 3, X, Y, Cybertruck.
safetyImpact: CRITICAL - potential shock hazard. Vehicle may enter limp mode or refuse to operate.

fixSteps.title: DO NOT self-service HV system
fixSteps.instruction: Arrange Tesla mobile service or tow to service center.

fixSteps.title: Service center diagnosis
